80	\section{The morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from two global sections}
81	\begin{lemma}
82	[Morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from a globally generating pair of sections]
83	  \label{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}
84	  
85	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}
86	  Let \(X\) be a scheme over a field \(\bar k\), let \(\mathcal L\) be an invertible
87	  sheaf on \(X\), and let \(s_0, s_1 \in H^0(X, \mathcal L)\) be two global
88	  sections whose common zero locus is empty:
89	  \[
90	    \{x \in X : s_0(x) = 0 \text{ and } s_1(x) = 0\} \;=\; \emptyset.
91	  \]
92	  (Equivalently, \(s_0\) and \(s_1\) generate \(\mathcal L\) at every point.) Then
93	  there exists a unique morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
94	  \[
95	    \varphi_{(s_0, s_1)} \colon X \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
96	  \]
97	  whose pullback of \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(1)\) is \(\mathcal L\) and whose
98	  pullbacks of the standard coordinate sections $x_0, x_1 \in
99	  H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal O(1))\( are \)s_0\( and \)s_1$ respectively. On the
100	  basic-open chart $D_+(x_0) = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k} \subset
101	  \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\( the morphism \)\varphi_{(s_0, s_1)}\( is the map ``\)x
102	  \mapsto s_1(x) / s_0(x)\(''; on the chart \)D_+(x_1)\( it is the map ``\)x
103	  \mapsto s_0(x) / s_1(x)$''; the two definitions agree on the overlap
104	  \(D_+(x_0) \cap D_+(x_1) = \mathbb G_{m, \bar k}\) by the cocycle identity
105	  \((s_1 / s_0) \cdot (s_0 / s_1) = 1\).
106	\end{lemma}
107	\begin{proof}
108	  This is the standard linear-system construction in the form of
109	  Hartshorne's Theorem~II.7.1 (``Morphisms to \(\mathbb P^n\)''), specialised to
110	  \(n = 1\). Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the corresponding API
111	  under the name
112	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} in
113	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}:
114	  \begin{quote}
115	  \small
116	  \texttt{def Proj.fromOfGlobalSections \{\(\sigma\) A\} [CommRing A]
117	    [SetLike \(\sigma\) A] [AddSubgroupClass \(\sigma\) A] (\(\mathcal A\) :
118	    \(\mathbb N\) → \(\sigma\)) [GradedRing \(\mathcal A\)] \{X : Scheme\}\\
119	    \phantom{}\quad(f : A →+* X.presheaf.obj \(\top\))\\
120	    \phantom{}\quad(hf : Ideal.map f (HomogeneousIdeal.irrelevant \(\mathcal A\)).toIdeal = \(\top\)) :\\
121	    \phantom{}\quad(X \(\longrightarrow\) Proj \(\mathcal A\))}
122	  \end{quote}
123	  Specialising to \(\mathcal A = \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) with the standard $\mathbb
124	  N\(-grading by total degree, the inputs are a graded \)\bar k$-algebra
125	  homomorphism \(f \colon \bar k[x_0, x_1] \to H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) and a
126	  proof that the irrelevant ideal \((x_0, x_1) \cdot \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) maps to
127	  the unit ideal of \(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) (i.e.\ no common zeros). The
128	  resulting morphism lands in $\mathrm{Proj}\,\bar k[x_0, x_1] = \mathbb
129	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (Hartshorne~II, Example~7.1.3, the construction of \)\mathbb
130	  P^n\( as a \)\mathrm{Proj}\(). When \)\mathcal L$ is non-trivial the present
131	  lemma's signature is reduced to the Mathlib API by working on the open
132	  cover \(X = D(s_0) \cup D(s_1)\), where on each chart the sections become
133	  honest global functions (\(s_1 / s_0\) on \(D(s_0)\), \(s_0 / s_1\) on \(D(s_1)\),
134	  agreeing on the overlap). The chart-glue is performed by the
135	  project-bespoke wrapper
136	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}, whose body
137	  in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} chains
138	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} with the chart-restriction-and-glue
139	  pattern of \texttt{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}.
140	  Uniqueness follows from the standard separation argument for morphisms to
141	  a separated scheme: two morphisms \(\varphi, \psi \colon X \to \mathbb P^1\)
142	  with \(\varphi^* x_i = \psi^* x_i\) for \(i = 0, 1\) agree on the basic-open
143	  charts \(D_+(x_0), D_+(x_1)\) (where they pull back the affine coordinates to
144	  the same rational function), and these charts cover \(\mathbb P^1\), so the
145	  two morphisms agree globally by gluing.
146	\end{proof}
147	\section{Degree of a morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via its pole divisor}
148	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase Pin 2 corrective; supersedes iter-182 narrative).
149	% `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor \varphi` is the Weil divisor \(\varphi^*[\infty]\),
150	% defined as `positivePart (principal (\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))` in Lean.
151	% The supporting `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` def lives in
152	% `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` (\ref{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart}); the
153	% Hartshorne~II.6.9 identity that powers the existential degree statement
154	% lives there as \ref{lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}.
155	\begin{lemma}
156	[Degree of a non-constant morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via the preimage of \(\infty\)]
157	  \label{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor}
158	  
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor}
160	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S2, opening paragraph of "Hurwitz's Theorem"
161	  % (definition of the degree of a finite morphism of curves) (read from
162	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
163	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
164	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
165	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
166	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.2, p.~299, opening of \S2.}
167	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a non-constant morphism
168	  from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\). Then
169	  \(\varphi\) is finite, its function-field extension
170	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\) (the canonical
171	  \(\varphi\)-induced map, supplied as an
172	  \texttt{[Algebra (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField C.functionField]}
173	  instance argument on the Lean side) is finite, and there exists a Weil
174	  divisor \(D \in \Div(C)\) such that
175	  \[
176	    D \;=\; \varphi^*[\infty]
177	    \quad\text{and}\quad
178	    \deg(D) \;=\; [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,].
179	  \]
180	  Equivalently, the morphism \(\varphi\) satisfies
181	  \[
182	    [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,] \;=\;
183	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
184	                  \ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [P]\,\right),
185	  \]
186	  where \(t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)\) is a local parameter at \(\infty\)
187	  (equivalently \(t_\infty = 1/u\) for the standard affine coordinate \(u\) on
188	  \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)); and for any closed point
189	  \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) the same identification gives
190	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C):\bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\).
191	  \emph{Convention on the pole divisor.} The Weil divisor
192	  \(\varphi^*[\infty]\) is exposed in Lean as
193	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor \(\varphi\)}, a
194	  noncomputable definition introduced (as a typed \texttt{sorry}) at the
195	  iter-182 plan-phase signature strengthening of this lemma. The body of
196	  \texttt{poleDivisor} is queued for iter-183+ closure via the standard
197	  affine-chart construction (\texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia}
198	  in Mathlib, applied at each closed point \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\)
199	  to read off the local ramification multiplicity
200	  \(\ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty)\) from the maximal ideal at \(\infty\) in
201	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)). On the Lean side, the existential output of
202	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} packages
203	  \(D = \texttt{Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor}\;\varphi\) together with
204	  \(\deg(D) = \operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)\),
205	  mirroring the OCofP pattern in which a typed-sorry def
206	  (\texttt{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}) is introduced
207	  upstream of its consumer lemma.
208	\end{lemma}
209	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase, supersedes iter-189 review NOTE):
210	% Pin 2 corrective COMMITTED — Option (a). `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` is
211	% being refactored to return the *positive part* of the principal divisor
212	% of `\varphi^{\#} t_\infty`, i.e. `(div(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))_0
213	% = \varphi^*[\infty]`. The supporting Weil-divisor `positivePart`
214	% definition lives in `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` as
215	% `def:WeilDivisor_positivePart`; the substantive Hartshorne~II.6.9
216	% identity that powers `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` is now pinned as
217	% `lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` in `WeilDivisor.tex`.
218	% With these substrates in place, `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` is
219	% mathematically correct as stated: the body's existential witness `D`
220	% is `Hom.poleDivisor \varphi = positivePart (principal (\varphi^{\#}
221	% t_\infty))`, and the degree identity is a one-line corollary of
222	% `lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`. iter-190 prover lane
223	% (Lane I) implements the refactor:
224	%   1. New def `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` in `WeilDivisor.lean`
225	%      (~10-15 LOC; lattice `D \sqcup 0`).
226	%   2. New lemma
227	%      `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` as a
228	%      named typed-sorry pin in `WeilDivisor.lean` (~5 LOC signature; body
229	%      ~50-80 LOC via affine-chart `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` route
230	%      owed iter-191+).
231	%   3. Refactor `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` body in `RationalCurveIso.lean`
232	%      from `principal (algebraMap _ _ t.val) halg` to
233	%      `positivePart (principal (algebraMap _ _ t.val) halg)` (~5 LOC).
234	%   4. Close `Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank` body via a 1-line
235	%      rewrite consuming (2) (~3 LOC).
236	% Net iter-190 Lane I: 1 axiom-clean closure (Pin 2 body, modulo named
237	% typed sorry on (2)); 1 NEW named typed-sorry pin
238	% (`degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`) which is itself an
239	% iter-191+ Hartshorne II.6.9 substrate target. See iter-190 sidecar
240	% `iter/iter-190/plan.md` for the full rationale.
241	\begin{proof}
242	  \uses{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart, lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank, def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_degree, thm:principal_deg_zero}
243	  A non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves over \(\bar k\) is
244	  automatically finite. Indeed, the image of \(\varphi\) is a closed
245	  irreducible subset of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) that is not a point (else
246	  \(\varphi\) would be constant) and not empty, so it is all of $\mathbb
247	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (the only proper irreducible closed subset of \)\mathbb
248	  P^1_{\bar k}\( is a point); hence \)\varphi$ is surjective. A surjective
249	  morphism between smooth proper irreducible curves of equal dimension is
250	  finite (proper + quasi-finite, where quasi-finiteness comes from the
251	  fact that \(\varphi\) has zero-dimensional fibres: a positive-dimensional
252	  fibre would force a one-dimensional irreducible component in the fibre,
253	  contradicting irreducibility of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(C\) at
254	  generic points). Hence the function-field extension
255	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) = \varphi^\# K(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\)
256	  is finite.
257	  The identification \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\) for
258	  any closed point \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) is the multiplicativity of the
259	  degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9): for a finite morphism
260	  \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) of smooth proper curves and any divisor $D \in
261	  \Div(C')\(, \)\deg(\varphi^* D) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg(D)$. Specialising
262	  to \(D = [Q]\) (a divisor of degree \(1\) since \(\kappa(Q) = \bar k\)) gives
263	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = \deg(\varphi)\). Equivalently, the degree of
264	  \(\varphi\) equals the number of preimages of any closed point of $\mathbb
265	  P^1\(, counted with the ramification multiplicities \)\ord_P(\varphi^*
266	  t_\infty)\( at the preimage points \)P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)$.
267	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The lemma threads two auxiliary inputs:
268	  ``non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves is finite'' (the
269	  surjective-and-proper-and-quasi-finite argument above; Hartshorne~II.6.8)
270	  and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback'' (Hartshorne~II.6.9
271	  and the multiplicativity of \(\deg\) under composition of \(\div\) and
272	  \(\deg\); the closely related ``\(\mathrm{div}(f)\) has degree zero'' specialisation
273	  is \texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}). Both are separable
274	  sub-lemmas inside \texttt{RR.4}'s Lean file
275	  (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) and are
276	  slotted for closure in a follow-up iteration after the
277	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} skeleton lands.
278	\end{proof}
279	\section{A degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism}
280	% NOTE (iter-181 Lane I signature refinement; see analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md
281	% Decision 2, verdict DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY). The iter-177 file-skeleton
282	% placeholder hypothesis "Nonempty (C'.functionField ≃+* C.functionField)"
283	% (an abstract function-field iso, unrelated to \(\varphi\)) was strictly
284	% weaker than the birational-extension body needs and was refined in
285	% iter-181 to the explicit function-field-extension-degree formulation
286	% [K(C):K(C')] = 1, i.e. Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField
287	% = 1, with C.functionField a C'.functionField-algebra via the canonical
288	% \varphi-induced map (Scheme.Hom.stalkMap at the generic point composed
289	% with IsFractionRing.lift). The proof-prose body below (the Hartshorne
290	% I.6.12 chain + scheme-theoretic alternative argument) is unchanged ---
291	% only the input shape changes.
292	\begin{lemma}
293	[Degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism]
294	  \label{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso}
295	  
296	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso_of_degree_one}
297	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], I \S6 Corollary~6.12 (function-field-to-curve
298	  % equivalence of categories) (read from
299	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 62, doc p.~45)
300	  % + [Hartshorne], IV \S2 opening paragraph (degree of a finite morphism
301	  % of curves is the function-field extension degree) (read from
302	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
303	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.12. \emph{The following three categories are
304	  % equivalent:}
305	  % (i) \emph{nonsingular projective curves, and dominant morphisms;}
306	  % (ii) \emph{quasi-projective curves, and dominant rational maps;}
307	  % (iii) \emph{function fields of dimension 1 over \(k\), and \(k\)-homomorphisms.}"
308	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
309	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
310	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
311	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, I.6 Corollary~6.12 (p.~45) + IV.2 opening
312	  (p.~299).}
313	  Let \(C, C'\) be smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves over an
314	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) be
315	  a non-constant morphism whose induced function-field extension is of
316	  degree one,
317	  \[
318	    [\,K(C) : K(C')\,] \;=\; 1,
319	  \]
320	  formally
321	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(C')} K(C) = 1\) in the Lean signature.
322	  Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
323	  \emph{Convention on the function-field map.} At each call-site of this
324	  lemma, the \(K(C')\)-algebra structure on \(K(C)\) used to interpret the
325	  degree \([K(C) : K(C')]\) is the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
326	  function-field map, i.e.\ the composite
327	  \[
328	    K(C') \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{germ}\;}\;
329	    \mathcal O_{C', \varphi(\xi_C)}
330	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\varphi^\#\;}\;
331	    \mathcal O_{C, \xi_C}
332	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{frac.}\,\text{lift}\;}\; K(C)
333	  \]
334	  of the germ at the image of the generic point \(\xi_C \in C\) with the
335	  stalk-map of \(\varphi\) at \(\xi_C\) and the fraction-field lift at
336	  \(\xi_C\) (per \texttt{analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md}, Decision~2). On
337	  the Lean side this canonical instance is carried as a
338	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
339	  to \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one}; consumers
340	  supply it explicitly at each call-site.
341	\end{lemma}
342	% NOTE (iter-189 review): Pin 3 Step 1 (function-field iso K(C') ≃+* K(C)
343	% from `Module.finrank = 1`) closed axiom-clean inline iter-189 (~10 LOC via
344	% `Subalgebra.bot_eq_top_of_finrank_eq_one` + `Algebra.surjective_algebraMap_iff`
345	% + `RingHom.injective` + `RingEquiv.ofBijective`). Step 2 (scheme-level lift
346	% via `Scheme.Hom.toNormalization`) remains a typed sorry in the body, with 4
347	% sub-obligations (a)-(d) documented inline at the sorry. Iter-190+ closure
348	% budget ~80-150 LOC per `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md`.
349	\begin{proof}
350	  The function-field hypothesis \([K(C) : K(C')] = 1\) states that the
351	  canonical \(\varphi\)-induced map \(\varphi^\# \colon K(C') \to K(C)\) is a
352	  finite extension of degree one; since \(K(C')\) is a field and the
353	  extension has dimension one as a \(K(C')\)-vector space, \(\varphi^\#\) is
354	  an isomorphism of function fields \(K(C') \cong K(C)\). (Equivalently, by
355	  the IV.2-opening identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C):K(C')]\), this is
356	  the statement \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\); the morphism \(\varphi\) is finite in
357	  this case by the standard argument that a non-constant morphism of
358	  smooth proper curves is finite, identical to the
359	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} preamble.)
360	  By Hartshorne's Corollary~I.6.12, the assignment \(C \mapsto \bar k(C)\) is
361	  an equivalence of categories between smooth projective curves over $\bar
362	  k$ (with dominant morphisms) and function fields of transcendence degree
363	  one over \(\bar k\) (with \(\bar k\)-homomorphisms). Under this equivalence,
364	  the isomorphism \(\bar k(C') \cong \bar k(C)\) produced by the previous
365	  paragraph lifts to a unique isomorphism of smooth proper curves $C \to
366	  C'\(, and this isomorphism agrees with \)\varphi$ on the generic point (by
367	  construction: it is the inverse-image of \(\varphi^\#\) under the
368	  equivalence). The two morphisms \(C \to C'\) that agree on the generic
369	  point of the integral scheme \(C\) agree everywhere (a morphism out of an
370	  integral scheme to a separated scheme is determined by its restriction
371	  to the generic point, Hartshorne~II Ex.~4.2). Hence \(\varphi\) is itself
372	  the lift, and is therefore an isomorphism.
373	  \emph{Alternative direct argument.} Without invoking the
374	  equivalence-of-categories framing, the same conclusion is obtained
375	  scheme-theoretically: a finite degree-\(1\) morphism $\varphi \colon C \to
376	  C'$ between integral normal noetherian schemes is an isomorphism.
377	  Indeed, the direct image \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C\) is a coherent
378	  \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module that is generically of rank $\deg(\varphi) =
379	  1\(, and an inclusion \)\mathcal O_{C'} \hookrightarrow \varphi_*
380	  \mathcal O_C\( of coherent rank-\)1$ sheaves whose generic ranks agree
381	  is an isomorphism over the locus where the quotient
382	  \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C / \mathcal O_{C'}\) has trivial stalk. The
383	  quotient is a torsion coherent \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module of generic rank
384	  \(\deg(\varphi) - 1 = 0\), hence is itself torsion-free (a
385	  finitely-generated torsion module over a Dedekind domain of generic
386	  rank zero is zero), so vanishes. Therefore $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C =
387	  \mathcal O_{C'}\( and \)\varphi$ is the structure morphism of an
388	  isomorphism. Cf.\ Stacks tag~\textbf{0AVX} for the categorical version.
389	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The Lean signature
390	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one} consumes a
391	  non-constant morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) together with a
392	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
393	  (the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced function-field map) and the
394	  function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis
395	  \texttt{Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField = 1}, and
396	  produces an isomorphism \(C \cong C'\) in the slice category
397	  \(\mathtt{Over (Spec (.of\ kbar))}\). The proof body threads the Mathlib
398	  API \texttt{IsIntegralHom.iso\_of\_isUnit} (or the equivalent
399	  \texttt{IsAffineHom + Finite + RankOne} chain) and, where needed, the
400	  project-bespoke ``finite-morphism-of-degree-one is an isomorphism''
401	  helper. The Hartshorne~I.6.12 equivalence-of-categories framing is the
402	  intended high-level proof structure; the scheme-theoretic argument
403	  above is the spelled-out form for the Lean body.
404	\end{proof}
405	\section{Headline: genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)}
406	\begin{theorem}
407	
408	\leanok
409	[Genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)]
410	  \label{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}
411	  
412	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
413	  % already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely
414	  % suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two distinct
415	  % points of \(X\) and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor \(D = P - Q\).
416	  % Since \(\deg(K - D) = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have \(l(K - D) = 0\),
417	  % and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of degree 0, so by
418	  % (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words \(P \sim Q\). But this implies
419	  % \(X\) is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
420	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
421	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, and let \(C\) be a smooth
422	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\).
423	  Then there exists an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
424	  \[
425	    \varphi \colon C \stackrel{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}.
426	  \]
427	  Concretely, the Lean signature
428	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} (the existing pinned
429	  target at \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290}) is
430	  closed by the proof below; the existing interface block
431	  \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} of
432	  \texttt{chap:avr_for_rr} is its consumer-side restatement,
433	  pinning the same Lean declaration.
434	\end{theorem}
435	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the
436	% statement block above (Hartshorne states the classification and its
437	% proof inside the single Example; the proof is the second half beginning
438	% "We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) \ldots"). The present
439	% proof body restates Hartshorne's argument in the project's notation
440	% (linear-system construction \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C
441	% ([P]))\(, degree-\)1$ identification, isomorphism).
442	\begin{proof}
443	  Over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) the smooth proper
444	  geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) has closed points, all of which are
445	  \(\bar k\)-rational (the residue field of a closed point of a curve over
446	  \(\bar k\) is a finite extension of \(\bar k\), hence is \(\bar k\) itself by
447	  algebraic closedness). Fix one such \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P \in C\);
448	  this is the marked point used to build the linear system below.
449	  \emph{Step 1: non-constant function \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\).}
450	  By Hartshorne's Riemann--Roch in genus zero
451	  (\texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}) applied to the
452	  effective divisor \([P]\) of degree \(1\), we obtain
453	  \(\ell([P]) = \deg([P]) + 1 = 2\). The constant section
454	  \(1 \in \bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) accounts for a
455	  one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-subspace; the two-dimensional quotient supplies
456	  a non-constant section $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
457	  \bar k \cdot 1$. This is the corollary
458	  \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
459	  chapter, \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OCofP.tex}).
460	  \emph{Step 2: morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via the linear system \((1, f)\).}
461	  The pair \((1, f) \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))^2\) generates the
462	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) at every point of \(C\): at points
463	  \(Q \in C \setminus \{P\}\) the section \(1\) does not vanish in
464	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])_Q \cong \mathcal O_{C, Q}\), and at the marked point
465	  \(P\) the section \(f\) does not vanish in \(\mathcal O_C([P])_P\) (because \(f\)
466	  has a pole of order exactly \(1\) at \(P\) in the rational-function
467	  picture: \(f \in K(C) \setminus \bar k\) with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = [P]\)).
468	  Therefore by \texttt{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections} there
469	  exists a unique morphism
470	  \[
471	    \varphi \;:=\; \varphi_{(1, f)} \colon C \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
472	  \]
473	  with \(\varphi^* x_0 = 1\) and \(\varphi^* x_1 = f\) (so the pullback of
474	  the standard ratio coordinate \(u = x_1 / x_0\) on $\mathbb P^1 \setminus
475	  \{\infty\}\( is \)f$, and the pullback of the inverse coordinate
476	  \(t_\infty = x_0 / x_1 = 1 / f\) is the local parameter \(1/f\) at \(P\)).
477	  \emph{Step 3: \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\) via the pole divisor of \(f\).} The
478	  morphism \(\varphi\) is non-constant (since \(f\) is non-constant as a
479	  rational function on \(C\), the pullback \(\varphi^* u = f\) takes
480	  infinitely many values, ruling out a constant \(\varphi\)). By
481	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} applied to \(\varphi\),
482	  \[
483	    \deg(\varphi)
484	    \;=\;
485	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{Q \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
486	                  \ord_Q(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [Q]\,\right).
487	  \]
488	  We compute the pole divisor of \(f\) as a rational function on \(C\):
489	  since \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) with \(f\) non-constant and
490	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) the line bundle of the effective divisor \([P]\), the
491	  rational-function \(f\) has pole divisor \((f)_\infty = [P]\) (the
492	  unique pole of order \(1\) at \(P\), no other poles; this is the standard
493	  identification of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\) with the rational functions
494	  \(g \in K(C)\) satisfying \(\mathrm{div}(g) + D \geq 0\), specialised to
495	  \(D = [P]\)). Hence \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty) = \{P\}\) with multiplicity~\(1\)
496	  and
497	  \[
498	    \deg(\varphi) \;=\; \deg([P]) \;=\; 1.
499	  \]
500	  \emph{Step 4: \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.} By Hartshorne's IV.2
501	  identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\), the
502	  degree-one conclusion of Step~3 rewrites as
503	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C) = 1\) (with
504	  the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
505	  \texttt{[Algebra \,(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField\,
506	   C.functionField]} instance). By
507	  \texttt{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} applied to the non-constant
508	  morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (smooth proper
509	  geometrically irreducible curves over \(\bar k\) on both sides), under
510	  this function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis, \(\varphi\) is an
511	  isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
512	  This produces the required isomorphism \(C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)
513	  closing the headline target
514	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}.
515	  \emph{Comparison with Hartshorne's original argument.} Hartshorne's
516	  Example~IV.1.3.5 phrases the argument in terms of two distinct points
517	  \(P, Q \in C\) and the degree-zero divisor \(D = P - Q\), deriving \(P \sim Q\)
518	  and concluding via II.6.10.1 that \(C\) is rational; combined with I.6.12
519	  this yields \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\). The present proof restates the same
520	  combinatorial content via the more directly formalisable linear-system
521	  construction: instead of going through the \(P \sim Q\) statement and
522	  invoking II.6.10.1 as a black box, we produce the explicit morphism
523	  \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1\) from the two sections \((1, f)\) of
524	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) given by Riemann--Roch and identify its degree
525	  directly. The two arguments are mathematically equivalent and have the
526	  same dependency footprint (Riemann--Roch in genus zero \(\Rightarrow\)
527	  \(\ell([P]) = 2\) \(\Rightarrow\) non-constant \(f\) \(\Rightarrow\) degree-\(1\)
528	  map to \(\mathbb P^1\) \(\Rightarrow\) iso); the linear-system phrasing maps
529	  more cleanly onto the Mathlib \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} API and
530	  the project's existing Weil-divisor/degree pipeline of \texttt{RR.1}.
531	\end{proof}

533	[Drop of \texttt{sorryAx} on the genus-\(0\) Albanese keystone]
534	  \label{rmk:rr4_unblocks_sorryAx_on_rigidity_genus0}

543	  \texttt{morphism\_P1\_to\_grpScheme\_const}'s residual sorries, tracked

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~REF below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (Definition~REF and Lemma~REF),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~REF),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~REF),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~REF), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~REF).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~REF) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~REF to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let \(R\) be a ring, and \(I \subset R\) an ideal. Let \(M\) be a finite \(R\)-module.
74	  %  The {\it \(I\)-depth} of \(M\), denoted \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if \(IM \not = M\), then \(\text{depth}_I(M)\) is the supremum in
77	  %  \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences in \(I\),
78	  %  \item if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\).
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If \((R, \mathfrak m)\) is local we call \(\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)\) simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of \(M\)."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(I \subseteq R\) be an ideal, and let \(M\) be a finitely
84	  generated \(R\)-module. The \emph{\(I\)-depth} of \(M\), written \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is the
85	  supremum in \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences contained
86	  in \(I\), provided \(IM \neq M\); if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\). When
87	  \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  \(\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\) and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of \(M\). The depth of the ring \(R\) itself is \(\text{depth}(R)\), the depth
90	  of \(R\) as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence \(f_1, \dots, f_r \in R\) is called \emph{\(M\)-regular} if
94	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0\) and for each \(i\), the element \(f_i\) is a non-zero-divisor on
95	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M\). The empty sequence is regular on \(M\) iff \(M \neq 0\) (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention \(\text{depth}(0) = \infty\)
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the \(\text{Ext}\) characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via \(\text{Ext}\)]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  
110	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
111	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\).
114	  %  Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module. Then \(\text{depth}(M)\)
115	  %  is equal to the smallest integer \(i\) such that
116	  %  \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\) is nonzero."
117	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
118	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
119	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}\) and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely generated \(R\)-module. Then
120	  \[
121	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
122	  \]
123	\end{lemma}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
126	% "Let \(\delta(M)\) denote the depth of \(M\) and let \(i(M)\) denote
127	%  the smallest integer \(i\) such that \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\)
128	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that \(i(M) < \infty\).
129	%  By Lemma REF we have
130	%  \(\delta(M) = 0\) if and only if \(i(M) = 0\), because
131	%  \(\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)\) exactly means
132	%  that \(i(M) = 0\). Hence if \(\delta(M)\) or \(i(M)\) is \(> 0\), then we may
133	%  choose \(x \in \mathfrak m\) such that (a) \(x\) is a nonzerodivisor
134	%  on \(M\), and (b) \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1\).
135	%  Consider the long exact sequence
136	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
137	%  \(0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0\) by Lemma REF:
138	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
139	%  Since \(x \in \mathfrak m\) all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
140	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
141	%  Lemma REF.
142	%  Thus it is clear that \(i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1\). Induction on
143	%  \(\delta(M)\) finishes the proof."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	  Write \(\delta := \text{depth}(M)\) and \(i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}\).
148	  The base case \(\delta = 0\) is exactly \(\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0\) (some element of
149	  \(M\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\), i.e.\ \(\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)\), i.e.\
150	  \(\mathfrak{m}\) contains a zero-divisor on \(M\)), which is \(i = 0\). For the inductive step
151	  pick \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) a non-zero-divisor on \(M\) with
152	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1\). The short exact sequence
153	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0\) induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
154	  -)\(. Multiplication by \)x\( annihilates each \)\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
155	  \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) and \(\kappa\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\)), so the long exact
156	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
157	  \(\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)\) at the relevant
158	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by \(1\). By induction
159	  \(i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1\), so \(i = \delta\).
160	\end{proof}
161	
162	\section{Projective dimension}
163	\label{sec:ab_pd}
164	
165	\begin{definition}
166	
167	\leanok
168	  [Projective dimension]
169	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
170	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
171	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
172	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
173	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
174	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
175	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
176	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
177	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module. The \emph{projective
178	  dimension} of \(M\), written \(\text{pd}_R(M)\), is the infimum in
179	  \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths \(n\) of projective resolutions
180	  \[
181	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
182	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
183	  \]
184	  with each \(P_i\) a projective \(R\)-module. We say \(M\) has \emph{finite projective dimension}
185	  if \(\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty\), and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
186	  convention \(\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty\) (some references); we use \(\text{pd}_R(0) = 0\) here
187	  (the empty resolution \(0 \to 0\) of length \(0\)), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
188	  Theorem~REF concerns only nonzero \(M\).
189	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated \(R\)-module of finite projective
190	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each \(P_i\)
191	  free of finite rank and each transition map \(P_{i+1} \to P_i\) landing in \(\mathfrak{m} P_i\).
192	\end{definition}
193	
194	The two basic facts about \(\text{pd}_R(M)\) needed downstream are:
195	\begin{itemize}
196	  \item \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) iff \(M\) is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
197	    iff \(M\) is free);
198	  \item if \(0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0\) is a short exact sequence with \(P\) projective, then
199	    \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1\) provided \(K \neq 0\).
200	\end{itemize}
201	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
202	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.
203	
204	\section{Depth on a short exact sequence}
205	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}
206	
207	\begin{lemma}
208	
209	\leanok
210	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
211	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
212	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_of_short_exact}
213	  
214	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
215	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
216	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
217	  % "Let \(R\) be a local Noetherian ring. Let \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\)
218	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite \(R\)-modules.
219	  %  \begin{enumerate}
220	  %  \item
221	  %  \(\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}\)
222	  %  \item
223	  %  \(\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}\)
224	  %  \item
225	  %  \(\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}\)
226	  %  \end{enumerate}"
227	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
228	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let
229	  \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
230	  \(R\)-modules. Then
231	  \begin{enumerate}
232	    \item \(\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)\);
233	    \item \(\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)\);
234	    \item \(\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)\).
235	  \end{enumerate}
236	\end{lemma}
237	
238	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
239	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
240	%  \(\Ext^i(\kappa, N)\), see Lemma REF,
241	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
242	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
243	%  from Lemma REF."
244	\begin{proof}
245	  
246	  
247	  By Lemma~REF, \(\text{depth}(N')\) (resp.\ \(\text{depth}(N)\),
248	  \(\text{depth}(N'')\)) is the smallest index at which \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) is
249	  nonzero on \(N'\) (resp.\ \(N\), \(N''\)). Apply the long exact \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\)
250	  sequence to \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\):
251	  \[
252	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
253	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
254	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
255	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
256	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
257	  \]
258	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
259	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
260	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
261	  the proof of Lemma~REF.
262	\end{proof}

264	\section{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula}
265	\label{sec:ab_main}
266	
267	\begin{theorem}
268	
269	\leanok
270	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum formula]
271	  \label{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
272	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula}
273	  
274	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum)
275	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L27207-L27280).
276	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
277	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring. Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module
278	  %  which has finite projective dimension \(\text{pd}_R(M)\). Then we have
279	  %  \(\)
280	  %  \text{depth}(R) = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)
281	  %  \(\)"
282	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum); cf.\
283	  Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Theorem 19.1; Auslander--Buchsbaum,
284	  \emph{Homological dimension in local rings}, 1957.}
285	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely
286	  generated \(R\)-module of finite projective dimension. Then
287	  \[
288	    \text{pd}_R(M) \;+\; \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \text{depth}(R).
289	  \]
290	\end{theorem}
291	
292	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
293	% "We prove this by induction on \(\text{depth}(M)\). The most interesting
294	%  case is the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\). In this case, let
295	%  \(\)
296	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
297	%  \(\)
298	%  be a minimal finite free resolution, so \(e = \text{pd}_R(M)\).
299	%  By Lemma REF we may assume all matrix
300	%  coefficients of the maps in the complex are contained in the maximal
301	%  ideal of \(R\). Then on the one hand, by
302	%  Proposition REF we see that
303	%  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, breaking the long
304	%  exact sequence into short exact sequences
305	%  \begin{align*}
306	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e - 1}} \to K_{e - 2} \to 0,\\
307	%  0 \to K_{e - 2} \to R^{n_{e - 2}} \to K_{e - 3} \to 0,\\
308	%  \ldots,\\
309	%  0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
310	%  \end{align*}
311	%  we see, using Lemma REF, that
312	%  \begin{align*}
313	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1,\\
314	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2,\\
315	%  \ldots,\\
316	%  \text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e - 1),\\
317	%  \text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e
318	%  \end{align*}
319	%  and since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we conclude \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\).
320	%  This finishes the proof of the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).
321	%
322	%  Induction step. If \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), then we pick \(x \in \mathfrak m\)
323	%  which is a nonzerodivisor on both \(M\) and \(R\). This is possible, because
324	%  either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\) and \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the aforementioned
325	%  Proposition REF or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which
326	%  case \(M\) is finite free hence also \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\).
327	%  Thus \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by Lemma REF
328	%  (for example) and we can find an \(x \in \mathfrak m\) which is a nonzerodivisor
329	%  on both \(R\) and \(M\). Let
330	%  \(\)
331	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
332	%  \(\)
333	%  be a minimal resolution as above. An application of the snake lemma
334	%  shows that
335	%  \(\)
336	%  0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to
337	%  M/xM \to 0
338	%  \(\)
339	%  is a minimal resolution too. Thus \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\).
340	%  By Lemma REF we have
341	%  \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
342	%  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\).
343	%  Till now depths have all been depths as \(R\) modules, but we observe that
344	%  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) and similarly for \(R/xR\).
345	%  By induction hypothesis we see that the
346	%  Auslander-Buchsbaum formula holds for \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\). Since the
347	%  depths of both \(R/xR\) and \(M/xM\) have decreased by one and the projective
348	%  dimension has not changed we conclude."
349	\begin{proof}
350	  
351	  We induct on \(\text{depth}(M)\).
352	
353	  \emph{Base case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).} Let \(e := \text{pd}_R(M)\) and pick a minimal
354	  finite free resolution
355	  \[
356	    0 \longrightarrow R^{n_e} \longrightarrow R^{n_{e-1}} \longrightarrow
357	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
358	  \]
359	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in \(\mathfrak{m}\) (this is the
360	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
361	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
362	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
363	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of \(r\)-minors) gives
364	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
365	  sequences
366	  \[
367	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
368	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
369	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
370	  \]
371	  and applying Lemma~REF iteratively,
372	  yields the chain of inequalities
373	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1\),
374	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2\), \dots,
375	  \(\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)\), and finally
376	  \(\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e\). Since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we deduce
377	  \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\). Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
378	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
379	
380	  \emph{Inductive step \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\).} We claim there exists \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\)
381	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both \(R\) and \(M\). Indeed: either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\), in
382	  which case \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
383	  of a minimal resolution, or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which case \(M\) is finite free and
384	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\). Either way both \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) and
385	  \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), hence \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by
386	  Lemma~REF, so a common non-zero-divisor $x \in
387	  \mathfrak{m}\( exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of \)M$ as above; the snake
388	  lemma applied to multiplication by \(x\) gives a minimal finite free resolution
389	  \[
390	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
391	  \]
392	  of \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\), of the same length \(e\). So
393	  \(\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\). By Stacks
394	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
395	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\), and one verifies
396	  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) (regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}\)
397	  correspond to regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}/(x)\)). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
398	  \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\):
399	  \[
400	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
401	  \]
402	  Substituting yields
403	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\), i.e.\
404	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\), as required.
405	\end{proof}

407	\section{Corollary: regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay}
408	\label{sec:ab_regular_cm}
409	
410	\begin{definition}
411	
412	\leanok
413	  [Cohen--Macaulay local ring]
414	  \label{def:cohen_macaulay_local}
415	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay}
416	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM)
417	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25288-L25299).
418	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
419	  % "A Noetherian local ring \(R\) is called {\it Cohen-Macaulay}
420	  %  if it is Cohen-Macaulay as a module over itself."
421	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM).}
422	  A Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is called \emph{Cohen--Macaulay} if
423	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\) (equivalently, if \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay as a module
424	  over itself, where Cohen--Macaulay for a finite \(R\)-module \(M\) means
425	  \(\dim(\text{Supp}(M)) = \text{depth}(M)\), cf.\ Stacks tag 00N3).
426	\end{definition}
427	
428	% NOTE (iter-185 review, supersedes iter-184): the Lean assembly
429	% `CohenMacaulay.of_regular` calls a non-private helper
430	% `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` (AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean
431	% L1096 — line drifted from iter-184's L944 reference) that now carries a
432	% structural induction scaffold via `regularLocal_inductive_step`, with the
433	% load-bearing sorry pushed into two named helpers: (i)
434	% `exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ` consolidating the
435	% Stacks 00NQ + 00NU substrate gaps in one signature, and (ii) an inline
436	% technical-bridge sorry inside `regularLocal_inductive_step` for the
437	% `R⧸(x)`-vs-`QuotSMulTop x R` linear-equiv upgrade (~10-20 LOC iter-186 close
438	% via `Submodule.ideal_span_singleton_smul` + `QuotSMulTop.mem_annihilator`).
439	% Neither has a `\lean{...}` pin in this chapter (the lean-vs-blueprint-checker
440	% iter185-auslander minor flagged adding a pin for the public-facing
441	% `exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` itself; iter-186+ chapter touch can add
442	% that hint). The Stacks 00NQ Mathlib gap remains the load-bearing substrate;
443	% iter-186+ work paths unchanged: (a) formalise Stacks 00NQ in project
444	% (~300 LOC); (b) Koszul-homology bypass via `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`;
445	% (c) Mathlib upstream.
446	\begin{corollary}
447	
448	\leanok
449	  [Regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay]
450	  \label{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
451	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular}
452	  
453	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM)
454	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25714-L25730).
455	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
456	  % "Let \(R\) be a regular local ring and let
457	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) be a minimal set of generators
458	  %  for the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\). Then
459	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) is a regular sequence, and
460	  %  each \(R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c)\) is a regular local ring
461	  %  of dimension \(d - c\). In particular \(R\) is Cohen-Macaulay."
462	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM).}
463	  Every regular Noetherian local ring is Cohen--Macaulay: if \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is
464	  regular local of Krull dimension \(d\), then \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\).
465	\end{corollary}
466	
467	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
468	% "Note that \(R/x_1R\) is a Noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\)
469	%  by Lemma REF with \(x_2, \ldots, x_d\)
470	%  generating the maximal ideal. Hence it is a regular local ring by definition.
471	%  Since \(R\) is a domain by Lemma REF
472	%  \(x_1\) is a nonzerodivisor."
473	\begin{proof}
474	  
475	  
476	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be regular of dimension \(d\) and pick a minimal generating set
477	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) of \(\mathfrak{m}\). By Stacks tag 00NQ (lemma-regular-domain), \(R\) is
478	  an integral domain, so \(x_1\) is a non-zero-divisor. The quotient \(R/x_1 R\) is a Noetherian
479	  local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\) (by Krull's principal ideal theorem,
480	  \texttt{lemma-one-equation}) with maximal ideal generated by \(x_2, \dots, x_d\), hence
481	  has dimension exactly \(d - 1\) and is again regular of dimension \(d - 1\). Inducting on \(d\),
482	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) is an \(R\)-regular sequence, so
483	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq d\). The reverse inequality \(\text{depth}(R) \leq \dim(R) = d\)
484	  always holds for any nonzero finite module over a Noetherian local ring
485	  (Stacks tag 00LK, \texttt{lemma-bound-depth}). Thus \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\), i.e.\
486	  \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay.
487	
488	  \emph{Remark on the Auslander--Buchsbaum route.} The same conclusion can be obtained from
489	  Theorem~REF as follows: take \(M := R\) itself. Then \(M\) is
490	  finitely generated and projective (free of rank \(1\)), so \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\). The
491	  formula gives \(\text{depth}(R) = 0 + \text{depth}(R)\), a tautology. The non-tautological
492	  use of Auslander--Buchsbaum is rather: for a regular local ring of dimension \(d\), every
493	  finitely generated module \(M\) has \(\text{pd}_R(M) \leq d\) (Stacks tag 00O2,
494	  \texttt{proposition-finite-gl-dim-regular}); applying the formula to such an \(M\) then
495	  relates \(\text{depth}(M)\) to \(d - \text{pd}_R(M)\), which is the form most often used in
496	  practice. The direct regular-sequence argument above is the cleanest proof of
497	  \(R\) Cohen--Macaulay itself.
498	\end{proof}
499	
500	\section{Application: codim-1 extension on a regular surface (gating A.4.a)}
501	\label{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}
502	
503	The downstream consumer is A.4.a: extending a rational map
504	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from a regular projective surface \(S\) to an abelian variety \(A\)
505	across a closed point \(x \in S\) that is missing from the domain of definition \(U \subseteq S\).
506	The classical mechanism (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.7) is:
507	
508	\begin{enumerate}
509	  \item The local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) is a regular Noetherian local ring of Krull
510	    dimension \(2\) (because \(S\) is regular and \(\dim S = 2\)).
511	  \item By Corollary~REF it is Cohen--Macaulay, so
512	    \(\text{depth}(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}) = 2\). Equivalently, the maximal ideal contains an
513	    \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\)-regular sequence of length \(2\) (any two transverse generators).
514	  \item Sections of the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S\) on \(U = S \setminus \{x\}\) extend
515	    uniquely across \(x\) because \(H^0_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) and
516	    \(H^1_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) (these local-cohomology vanishings are equivalent to depth
517	    \(\geq 2\) at \(x\) by Stacks tag 0AVF; see Hartshorne, III.3.5, for the surface case).
518	  \item For a rational map \(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) with codimension-\(\geq 2\) indeterminacy
519	    locus on a regular surface, the same depth-\(\geq 2\) input produces a sheaf-theoretic
520	    extension of \(f\) to all of \(S\): pull back any sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_A\)-affine-locally,
521	    obtain sections over \(U\), and extend across \(x\) by the above.
522	\end{enumerate}
523	
524	So Corollary~REF is the gating input: the geometric A.4.a
525	proof can be written conditional on the algebraic Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary, and the
526	two halves develop independently --- as long as Theorem~REF and
527	Corollary~REF are formalized in
528	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} with the signatures pinned
529	in this chapter, A.4.a can consume them as Mathlib-style imports.
530	
531	\section{Lean encoding}
532	\label{sec:ab_lean_encoding}
533	
534	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean}.
535	Mathlib at the project's pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}) already exposes substantial parts
536	of the depth / projective-dimension API:
537	\begin{itemize}
538	  \item \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.RegularSequence} provides

32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:quot_setup}
34	
35	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
36	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
37	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
38	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
39	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
40	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
41	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
42	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
43	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness; see~\)
44	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
45	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
46	
47	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1 and
48	% \S 5; FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
49	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500 +
50	% L2154-L2542). The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
51	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
52	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
53	% the verbatim source-quote in \ is taken from
54	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
55	% reference).
56	
57	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
58	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
59	
60	\begin{definition}\leanok
61	[Hilbert polynomial]
62	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
63	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
64	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
65	  % Polynomials" (read from
66	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
67	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
68	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
69	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
70	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
71	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
72	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
73	  %  the function
74	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
75	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
76	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
77	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
78	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
79	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
80	  %
81	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
82	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
83	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
84	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
85	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
86	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
87	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
88	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
89	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
90	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
91	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
92	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
93	  III.5.2.}
94	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
95	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
96	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), the
97	  \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\) relative to \(L\)} is the
98	  polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) characterised by
99	  \[
100	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
101	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
102	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
103	  \]
104	  (a polynomial in \(m\) by Snapper's Lemma; the sum is finite by coherence and
105	  properness). When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
106	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
107	  well-defined on connected components.
108	\end{definition}
109	
110	\section{The Quot functor}
111	\label{sec:quot_functor}
112	
113	\begin{definition}\leanok
114	[Quot functor]
115	  \label{def:quot_functor}
116	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
117	  
118	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
119	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
120	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
121	  % + L481-L498).
122	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
123	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
124	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
125	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
126	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
127	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
128	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
129	  %
130	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
131	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
132	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
133	  %
134	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
135	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
136	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
137	  %
138	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
139	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
140	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
141	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
142	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
143	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
144	  %  well-defined, which gives
145	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
146	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
147	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
148	  %  [...]
149	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
150	  %  as a co-product
151	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
152	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
153	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
154	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
155	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
156	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
157	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
158	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
159	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
160	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
161	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
162	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
163	  \[
164	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
165	  \]
166	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
167	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
168	  \begin{enumerate}
169	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
170	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) and which is flat over \(T\);
171	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
172	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
173	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
174	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
175	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
176	      \(\Phi\).
177	  \end{enumerate}
178	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
179	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
180	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
181	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
182	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
183	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
184	\end{definition}

186	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
187	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
188	
189	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
190	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
191	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
192	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
193	
194	\begin{definition}\leanok
195	[Grassmannian scheme]
196	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
197	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
198	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises~(2)" and
199	  % "Construction of Grassmannian" (read from
200	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L599
201	  % + L773-L850).
202	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
203	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), an explicit construction
204	  %  the Grassmannian scheme
205	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
206	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
207	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
208	  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]
209	  %  Show that \(\grass(r,d)\) together with the quotient
210	  %  \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) represents the
211	  %  contravariant functor
212	  %  \(\)\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}\(\)
213	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any \(T\) the
214	  %  set of all equivalence classes
215	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients \(q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F\)
216	  %  where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\).
217	  %  [...]
218	  %  Using the above show that, more generally, if \(S\) is a scheme and
219	  %  \(E\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\),
220	  %  the functor \(\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}\)
221	  %  on all \(S\)-schemes which
222	  %  by definition associates to any \(T\)
223	  %  the set of all equivalence classes \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients
224	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\),
225	  %  is representable. The representing scheme
226	  %  is denoted by \(\grass(E,d)\) and is called the rank \(d\)
227	  %  relative Grassmannian of \(E\) over \(S\)."
228	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) and "Construction of
229	  Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
230	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
231	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
232	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
233	  \[
234	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
235	    \qquad
236	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
237	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
238	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
239	  \]
240	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Concretely $\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) =
241	  \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\( (the Quot functor for \)X = S\(, \)E = V$,
242	  constant Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\)).
243	\end{definition}
244	
245	\begin{theorem}\leanok
246	[Representability of the Grassmannian]
247	  \label{thm:grassmannian_representable}
248	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian.representable}
249	  
250	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
251	  % affine patches" through "Projective embedding"
252	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
253	  % L798-L930).
254	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
255	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), the Grassmannian scheme
256	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
257	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
258	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
259	  %  can be explicitly constructed as follows. [...]
260	  %  the schemes
261	  %  \(U^I\), as \(I\) varies over all the
262	  %  \({r\choose d}\) different
263	  %  subsets of  \(\{ 1,\ldots, r\}\) of cardinality \(d\),
264	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle \((\theta_{I,J})\) to form a
265	  %  finite-type scheme \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\). As each \(U^I\) is
266	  %  isomorphic to \(\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}\), it follows that
267	  %  \(\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\) is smooth of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\).
268	  %  [...]
269	  %  We now show that \(\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\) is proper. It is enough to
270	  %  verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.
271	  %  [...]
272	  %  As \(\U\) is given by the transition functions \(g_{I,J}\) described above,
273	  %  the determinant line bundle
274	  %  \(\det(\U)\) is given by the transition functions
275	  %  \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J \in GL_1(U^I_J)\).
276	  %  [...]
277	  %  We leave it to the reader to verify that the sections \(\sigma_I\) form
278	  %  a linear system which is base point free and separates points
279	  %  relative to \(\spec \Z\), and so gives an embedding of
280	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) into \(\PP^m_{\Z}\) where \(m = {r\choose d}-1\).
281	  %  This is a closed embedding by the properness of
282	  %  \(\pi:\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\). In particular, \(\det(\U)\)
283	  %  is a relatively very ample line bundle on \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\)."
284	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Construction of Grassmannian" (FGA
285	  Explained Ch.~5).}
286	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(V\) a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module
287	  of rank \(r\), and \(1 \le d \le r\). The functor \(\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\) of
288	  \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} is representable by a smooth projective
289	  \(S\)-scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \to S\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\),
290	  equipped with a tautological quotient
291	  \(\pi^* V \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). The determinant
292	  line bundle \(\det(\mathcal{U})\) is relatively very ample, and the
293	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding
294	  $\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \hookrightarrow
295	  \mathbb{P}_S\!\left(\bigwedge^d V\right)$ -- the \emph{Pl\"ucker embedding}.
296	\end{theorem}
297	
298	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
299	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
300	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
301	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
302	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
303	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
304	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
305	\begin{proof}
306	  
307	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
308	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
309	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
310	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
311	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
312	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
313	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
314	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
315	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
316	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
317	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
318	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
319	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
320	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
321	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
322	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
323	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
324	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
325	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
326	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
327	
328	  The universal quotient $u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
329	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\( is given on \)U^I\( by the matrix \)X^I$
330	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
331	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
332	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
333	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
334	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
335	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
336	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
337	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
338	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
339	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
340	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
341	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
342	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism $T \to U^I \subset
343	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\(. Base change from \)\mathbb{Z}$ to a general noetherian
344	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
345	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
346	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
347	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
348	\end{proof}
