350	\section{Representability of the Quot scheme}
351	\label{sec:quot_representable}
352	
353	\begin{theorem}\leanok
354	[Representability of the Quot functor]
355	  \label{thm:quot_representable}
356	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotScheme}
357	  
358	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Main Existence Theorems"
359	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
360	  % L2220-L2273); cf.\ Grothendieck, FGA TDTE-IV.
361	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
362	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi: X\to S\) a projective morphism,
363	  %  and \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\). Then for any
364	  %  coherent \({\mathcal O}_X\)-module \(E\) and any polynomial \(\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]\),
365	  %  the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) is representable
366	  %  by a projective \(S\)-scheme \(\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\)."
367	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, Theorem (Grothendieck), Theorem
368	  (Altman--Kleiman) (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ Grothendieck, FGA TDTE-IV.}
369	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a projective morphism,
370	  \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent
371	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). Then the
372	  functor \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) of \cref{def:quot_functor} is
373	  representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\),
374	  equipped with a universal quotient
375	  \[
376	    q^{\mathrm{univ}} :
377	    \pi^*_{\Quot} E \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}^{\mathrm{univ}}
378	    \quad \text{on } X \times_S \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S},
379	  \]
380	  flat over \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) with constant Hilbert polynomial
381	  \(\Phi\) on every fiber. The Hilbert scheme is the special case
382	  \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\): \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} = \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\)
383	  represents the functor of \(T\)-flat closed subschemes
384	  \(Y \subset X_T\) with fiberwise Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\).
385	\end{theorem}
386	
387	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: this proof is long (~5 named sub-sections in [Nitsure]
388	% \S 5). Each sub-step is recorded with its own SOURCE QUOTE in the
389	% sub-lemmas below. The structural skeleton of the proof is restated in
390	% project notation in the \begin{proof} block.
391	\begin{proof}
392	  
393	  The proof has four steps; each step is a sub-lemma in the next
394	  sub-sections.
395	  \begin{enumerate}
396	    \item \textbf{(Boundedness, \cref{lem:quot_boundedness}.)} Pick the
397	      Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity bound $m = m(\mathrm{rank}\,V,
398	      \mathrm{rank}\,W, \Phi)$ uniformly across all fibers (by Mumford's
399	      \(m\)-regularity theorem applied to \(E_s\) and to all coherent
400	      quotients of \(E_s\) with Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\)). For \(r \ge m\)
401	      and any \(T \to S\) and any \(T\)-flat quotient
402	      \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) with Hilbert polynomial
403	      \(\Phi\), the higher direct images \(R^i (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\)
404	      and \(R^i (\pi_T)_* E_T(r)\) and \(R^i (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r)\)
405	      vanish for \(i \ge 1\) (where \(\mathcal{G} = \ker q\)), and the
406	      direct images \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\), \((\pi_T)_* E_T(r)\),
407	      \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r)\) are locally free of ranks fixed by the
408	      data; the canonical maps \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_*(-)(r) \to (-)(r)\)
409	      are surjective. Cohomology-and-base-change (\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology},
410	      Stacks tag 02KH) propagates these properties across \(T\)-base change.
411	    \item \textbf{(Grassmannian embedding, \cref{lem:quot_alpha_injective}.)}
412	      Reduce by \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W} to the case
413	      \(X = \mathbb{P}_S(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\) with \(V, W\) locally free
414	      on \(S\), \(L = \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}(V)}(1)\). Fix \(r \ge m\).
415	      The Grassmannian embedding
416	      \[
417	        \alpha : \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \longrightarrow
418	        \mathrm{Grass}\!\left(
419	          W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V,\; \Phi(r)
420	        \right)
421	      \]
422	      sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) on \(T\) to the locally free
423	      quotient \((\pi_T)_*(q(r)) : (\pi_T)_* E_T(r) \to (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\).
424	      This is injective on \(T\)-valued points: from the map $T \to
425	      \mathrm{Gr}_S(W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))$ one recovers
426	      the kernel-pull-back \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r) \to E_T(r)\),
427	      whose cokernel is \(q(r) : E_T(r) \to \mathcal{F}(r)\);
428	      twisting back by \(-r\) recovers \(q\).
429	    \item \textbf{(Locally closed in the Grassmannian via flattening
430	      stratification.)} Let $T_0 = \mathrm{Gr}_S(W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V,
431	      \Phi(r))$ with universal quotient
432	      \(f : (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V)_{T_0} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{J}\)
433	      and kernel \(\mathcal{K}\). Form the composite
434	      $h : \pi_{T_0}^* \mathcal{K} \hookrightarrow
435	      \pi_{T_0}^* (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V) = \pi_{T_0}^* (\pi_{T_0})_* E_{T_0}
436	      \twoheadrightarrow E_{T_0}$
437	      and let \(q := \mathrm{coker}(h) : E_{T_0} \to \mathcal{F}\). By the
438	      flattening stratification (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
439	      of \cref{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\)
440	      on \(X_{T_0}\), there exists a locally closed subscheme
441	      \(T_0^\Phi \subseteq T_0\) such that for any \(\phi : Y \to T_0\), the
442	      pulled-back cokernel \(\mathcal{F}_Y\) is \(Y\)-flat with fiberwise
443	      Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\) iff \(\phi\) factors through \(T_0^\Phi\).
444	      The pair \((T_0^\Phi, \text{universal cokernel})\) represents
445	      \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\).
446	    \item \textbf{(Closed, hence projective,
447	      \cref{lem:quot_valuative_criterion}.)} The functor
448	      \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) satisfies the valuative criterion of
449	      properness with respect to DVRs (Exercise (7) of \S 1, proved
450	      using that flatness over a DVR is torsion-freeness). Combined
451	      with the locally closed embedding of step 3 into the projective
452	      $\mathrm{Gr}_S \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^{\Phi(r)}
453	      (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V))$, this upgrades to a \emph{closed}
454	      embedding. Hence \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) is a projective \(S\)-scheme.
455	  \end{enumerate}
456	  Finally, by \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W}, the general case
457	  (arbitrary \(X\) projective over \(S\) and arbitrary coherent \(E\)) follows
458	  from the case \(X = \mathbb{P}(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\): pick a closed
459	  embedding \(X \subset \mathbb{P}(V)\) and write \(E\) as a coherent quotient
460	  of \(\pi^*(W)(\nu)\) for some vector bundle \(W\) on \(S\) and integer \(\nu\);
461	  the Quot scheme of the original data is then a closed subscheme of the
462	  Quot scheme of the universal data, by the closed-embedding clause of
463	  \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W}.
464	\end{proof}

466	\section{Sub-lemmas of the construction}
467	\label{sec:quot_sub_lemmas}
468	
469	\begin{lemma}[Reduction to the universal case]
470	  \label{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W}
471	  
472	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Reduction to the case of
473	  % \(\Quot_{\pi^*W/\PP(V)/S}^{\Phi,L}\)"
474	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
475	  % L2294-L2332).
476	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
477	  % "It is enough to prove Theorem REF in the special case
478	  %  that \(X = \PP(V)\) and
479	  %  \(E= \pi^*(W)\) where \(V\) and \(W\) are vector bundles on \(S\), as
480	  %  a consequence of the next lemma.
481	  %
482	  %  \begin{lemma}
483	  %  (i) Let \(\nu\) be any integer.
484	  %  Then tensoring by \(L^{\nu}\) gives an isomorphism of
485	  %  functors from \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) to
486	  %  \(\Quot_{E(\nu)/X/S}^{\Psi,L}\) where the polynomial
487	  %  \(\Psi \in \Q[\lambda]\) is defined by \(\Psi(\lambda) = \Phi(\lambda + \nu)\).
488	  %
489	  %  (ii) Let \(\phi : E \to G\) be a surjective homomorphism of
490	  %  coherent sheaves on \(X\). Then the corresponding natural transformation
491	  %  \(\Quot_{G/X/S}^{\Phi,L} \to \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\)
492	  %  is a closed embedding.
493	  %  \end{lemma}"
494	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
495	  Let \(S\), \(X\), \(L\), \(E\), \(\Phi\) be as in \cref{def:quot_functor}.
496	  \begin{enumerate}
497	    \item (Twist-shift.) For \(\nu \in \mathbb{Z}\), tensoring by
498	      \(L^{\otimes \nu}\) gives an isomorphism of functors
499	      $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \xrightarrow{\sim}
500	      \Quot^{\Psi,L}_{E(\nu)/X/S}\( with \)\Psi(\lambda) = \Phi(\lambda + \nu)$.
501	    \item (Surjection induces closed embedding.) Given a surjection
502	      \(\phi : E \twoheadrightarrow G\) of coherent sheaves on \(X\), the
503	      induced natural transformation
504	      \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{G/X/S} \to \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) is a closed
505	      embedding of functors.
506	  \end{enumerate}
507	  Consequently, if \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\pi^* W / \mathbb{P}(V)/S}\) is
508	  representable for all \(V\), \(W\) vector bundles on \(S\), then so is
509	  \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) for every \(X \subset \mathbb{P}(V)\) closed and
510	  every coherent \(E\) on \(X\) that is a quotient of \(\pi^*(W)(\nu)|_X\) for
511	  some \(W\) and \(\nu\).
512	\end{lemma}
513	
514	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The statement (i) is obvious. The statement (ii) just
515	% says that given any locally noetherian scheme \(T\) and a family
516	% \(\langle \F,q\rangle \in \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(T)\), there exists
517	% a closed subscheme \(T' \subset T\) with the following universal property:
518	% for any locally noetherian scheme \(U\) and a morphism \(f: U\to T\),
519	% the pulled back homomorphism of \({\mathcal O}_{X_U}\)-modules \(q_U : E_U \to \F_U\)
520	% factors via the pulled back homomorphism \(\phi_U : E_U \to G_U\)
521	% if and only if \(U \to T\) factors via \(T'\hra T\).
522	% This is satisfied by taking \(T'\) to be the vanishing scheme
523	% for the composite homomorphism \(\ker(\phi) \hra  E \stackrel{q}{\to} \F\)
524	% of coherent sheaves on \(X_T\) (see Remark REF),
525	% which makes sense here as both \(\ker(\phi)\) and \(\F\) are coherent
526	% on \(X_T\) and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\)."
527	\begin{proof}
528	  
529	  (i) is direct from the definitions: tensoring with \(L^{\otimes \nu}\) is
530	  an autoequivalence of coherent sheaves on \(X\) (and on every \(X_T\)),
531	  preserving surjectivity, \(T\)-flatness, and properness of support, and
532	  shifting the Hilbert polynomial by \(\nu\) in the variable.
533	
534	  (ii) Given a family \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) on \(T\) in
535	  \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), the locus where \(q\) factors through
536	  \(\phi : E \twoheadrightarrow G\) is cut out by the vanishing of the
537	  composite $\ker(\phi)|_{X_T} \hookrightarrow E_T \xrightarrow{q}
538	  \mathcal{F}\(. Because \)\mathcal{F}\( is \)T$-flat, the schematic image of
539	  this composite descends to a closed subscheme \(T' \subset T\) such that
540	  \(f : U \to T\) factors through \(T'\) iff \(q_U\) factors through \(\phi_U\).
541	  Hence \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{G/X/S} \hookrightarrow \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\)
542	  is a closed embedding of subfunctors.
543	\end{proof}

545	\begin{lemma}[Uniform Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity bound]
546	  \label{lem:quot_boundedness}
547	  
548	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Use of \(m\)-Regularity"
549	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
550	  % L2346-L2404). Builds on [Nitsure] \S 2 (Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity)
551	  % and \S 3 (semi-continuity and base-change), cited internally as
552	  % Theorem~"Mumford" and Theorem~"Base-change for flat sheaves".
553	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
554	  % "We consider the sheaf \(E = \pi^*(W)\) on \(X=\PP(V)\) where
555	  %  \(V\) is a vector bundle on \(S\),
556	  %  and take \(L = {\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)}(1)\).
557	  %  For any field \(k\) and a \(k\)-valued point
558	  %  \(s\) of \(S\), we have an isomorphism
559	  %  \(\PP(V)_s \simeq \PP^n_k\) where \(n=\rank(V)-1\),
560	  %  and the restricted sheaf \(E_s\) on \(\PP(V)_s\) is isomorphic to
561	  %  \(\oplus^p{\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)_s}\) where \(p=\rank(W)\).
562	  %  It follows from Theorem REF
563	  %  that given any \(\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]\), there exists an integer
564	  %  \(m\) which depends only on \(\rank(V)\), \(\rank(W)\) and \(\Phi\),
565	  %  such that for any field \(k\) and a \(k\)-valued point \(s\) of \(S\),
566	  %  the sheaf \(E_s\) on \(\PP(V)_s\) is \(m\)-regular, and for any
567	  %  coherent quotient \(q: E_s \to \F\) on \(\PP(V)_s\) with
568	  %  Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\), the sheaf \(\F\) and the kernel sheaf
569	  %  \(\G\subset E_s\) of \(q\) are both \(m\)-regular."
570	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Use of \(m\)-Regularity" (FGA Explained
571	  Ch.~5).}
572	  Let \(X = \mathbb{P}_S(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\) with \(V\), \(W\) vector bundles on
573	  \(S\) of ranks \(n+1\), \(p\), \(L = \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}(V)}(1)\), and
574	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). There exists an integer
575	  \(m = m(n, p, \Phi)\), depending only on \(n\), \(p\), and \(\Phi\), such that
576	  for every geometric point \(s\) of \(S\) and every coherent quotient
577	  \(q : E_s \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) with Hilbert polynomial
578	  \(\Phi\), the sheaves \(E_s\), \(\mathcal{F}\), and \(\ker q\) on \(X_s\) are all
579	  \(m\)-regular in the sense of Castelnuovo--Mumford. Consequently, for
580	  every \(T \to S\) and every \(T\)-flat quotient
581	  \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) on \(X_T\) with Hilbert
582	  polynomial \(\Phi\) and every \(r \ge m\), the conclusions \textbf{(*)} and
583	  \textbf{(**)} of \cite[\S 5, "Use of \(m\)-Regularity"]{nitsure-hilbert-quot}
584	  hold: the direct images \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\), \((\pi_T)_* E_T(r)\),
585	  \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r)\) are locally free of fixed ranks; the
586	  canonical surjections \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_*(-)(r) \twoheadrightarrow (-)(r)\)
587	  hold; and the higher direct images \(R^i (\pi_T)_* (-)(r)\) vanish for
588	  \(i \ge 1\).
589	\end{lemma}
590	
591	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see [Nitsure], \S 5, "Use of \(m\)-Regularity"
592	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L2346-L2404).
593	% The proof invokes the m-regularity theorem of [Nitsure] \S 2
594	% (Theorem "Mumford") to extract the uniform bound m, and the
595	% base-change-for-flat-sheaves theorem of [Nitsure] \S 3 (cited as
596	% "Base-change for flat sheaves") to propagate the conclusions across \(T\).
597	\begin{proof}
598	  
599	  Each geometric fiber \(E_s\) is a trivial bundle on \(\mathbb{P}^n_{\kappa(s)}\)
600	  of rank \(p\), so \(E_s\) is \(0\)-regular. The Castelnuovo--Mumford
601	  \(m\)-regularity theorem of [Nitsure] \S 2 produces, for any fixed Hilbert
602	  polynomial \(\Phi\), a uniform integer \(m = m(n, p, \Phi)\) above which
603	  every coherent quotient of \(E_s\) with Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\) -- and
604	  its kernel -- is \(m\)-regular. The Castelnuovo Lemma of [Nitsure] \S 2
605	  converts \(m\)-regularity into vanishing of \(H^i((-) (r))\) for \(i \ge 1\)
606	  and \(r \ge m\), and into global generation of \(H^0((-)(r))\). To
607	  propagate the fiber-wise statement to families over \(T\), apply the
608	  flat-base-change theorem (\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}, Stacks
609	  tag 02KH) along \(T \to S\): cohomology of flat families commutes with
610	  base change, so the direct images \((\pi_T)_*(-)(r)\) are locally free of
611	  the rank dictated by the fiber Hilbert polynomials, and the canonical
612	  maps \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_*(-)(r) \to (-)(r)\) inherit fiberwise
613	  surjectivity.
614	\end{proof}

616	\begin{lemma}[Grassmannian embedding \(\alpha\) is injective]
617	  \label{lem:quot_alpha_injective}
618	  
619	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Embedding Quot into Grassmannian"
620	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
621	  % L2409-L2451).
622	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
623	  % "We now fix a positive integer \(r\) such that \(r\ge m\).
624	  %  Note that the rank of \({\pi_T}_*\F(r)\) is \(\Phi(r)\)
625	  %  and \(\pi_*E(r) = W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V\).

627	  %  \({\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)\)
628	  %  defines an element of the set
629	  %  \(\Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V , \Phi(r))(T)\).
630	  %  We thus get a morphism of functors
631	  %  \(\)\alpha : \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \to
632	  %  \Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))\(\)
633	  %  It associates to \(q: E_T \to \F\) the quotient
634	  %  \({\pi_T}_*(q(r)) : {\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)\).
635	  %
636	  %  The above morphism \(\alpha\) is injective because the quotient

639	  %  as follows."
640	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Embedding Quot into Grassmannian"
641	  (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
642	  In the universal setup \(X = \mathbb{P}_S(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\), fix
643	  \(r \ge m\) from \cref{lem:quot_boundedness}. The natural transformation
644	  \[
645	    \alpha :
646	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \longrightarrow
647	    \mathrm{Grass}\!\left(
648	      W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V,\; \Phi(r)
649	    \right),
650	    \qquad
651	    \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle \longmapsto
652	    \langle (\pi_T)_*\mathcal{F}(r),\, (\pi_T)_*(q(r))\rangle
653	  \]
654	  is well-defined (the target is locally free of rank \(\Phi(r)\), and the
655	  canonical isomorphism $\pi_* E(r) \cong W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S}
656	  \mathrm{Sym}^r V\( gives the source) and \emph{injective} on \)T$-points:
657	  the data of the locally free quotient at level \(r\) determines
658	  \(q : E_T \to \mathcal{F}\).
659	\end{lemma}
660	
661	\begin{proof}
662	  
663	  Suppose \(\alpha(T)\) sends both \(\langle \mathcal{F}_1, q_1\rangle\) and
664	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}_2, q_2\rangle\) to the same \(T\)-point of the
665	  Grassmannian. Then the pulled-back kernel
666	  $(\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}_i(r) \to (\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* E_T(r)
667	  = \pi_{T_0}^* (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V)_T$
668	  is the same. The diagram \textbf{(**)} of
669	  \cref{lem:quot_boundedness} gives a right-exact sequence
670	  \[
671	    (\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}_i(r) \xrightarrow{h}
672	    E_T(r) \xrightarrow{q_i(r)} \mathcal{F}_i(r) \to 0,
673	  \]
674	  so \(q_i(r)\) is recovered as the cokernel of \(h\); twisting by \(-r\)
675	  recovers \(q_i\), giving \(q_1 = q_2\) and hence
676	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}_1, q_1\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}_2, q_2\rangle\).
677	\end{proof}
678	
679	\begin{lemma}[Valuative criterion of properness for \(\Quot\)]
680	  \label{lem:quot_valuative_criterion}
681	  
682	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Valuative Criterion for Properness"
683	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
684	  % L2510-L2542). Original reference: EGA IV(2) 2.8.1, cited there.
685	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
686	  % "The functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) satisfies the following valuative
687	  %  criterion for properness over \(S\): given any discrete valuation ring
688	  %  \(R\) over \(S\) with quotient field \(K\), the restriction map
689	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec R) \to \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec K)\(\)
690	  %  is bijective. This can be seen as follows.
691	  %  Given any coherent quotient
692	  %  \(q: E_K \to \F\) on \(X_R\) which defines an element \(\langle \F, q\rangle\)
693	  %  of \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec K)\).
694	  %  Let \(\ov{\F}\) be the image of the composite homomorphism
695	  %  \(E_R \to j_*(E_K) \to j_*\F\) where \(j : X_K \hra X_R\) is the open
696	  %  inclusion. Let \(\ov{q} : E_R\to \ov{\F}\) be the induced surjection.
697	  %  Then we leave it to the reader to verify that
698	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) is an element of \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec R)\)
699	  %  which maps to \(\langle \F, q\rangle\), and is the unique such element.
700	  %  (Use the basic fact that being flat over a dvr
701	  %  is the same as being torsion-free.)"
702	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Valuative Criterion for Properness"
703	  (FGA Explained Ch.~5); EGA IV(2) 2.8.1.}
704	  The functor \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) satisfies the valuative criterion
705	  for properness over \(S\): for every discrete valuation ring \(R\) with
706	  fraction field \(K\) and every morphism \(\Spec R \to S\), the restriction
707	  map
708	  \[
709	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}(\Spec R)
710	    \longrightarrow
711	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}(\Spec K)
712	  \]
713	  is bijective.
714	\end{lemma}
715	
716	\begin{proof}
717	  
718	  Given a \(\Spec K\)-point \(\langle \mathcal{F}_K, q_K\rangle\) on \(X_K\),
719	  let \(j : X_K \hookrightarrow X_R\) be the open inclusion. Define
720	  \(\overline{\mathcal{F}}\) as the image of the composite
721	  \(E_R \to j_* E_K \to j_* \mathcal{F}_K\), with induced surjection
722	  \(\overline{q} : E_R \to \overline{\mathcal{F}}\). The sheaf
723	  \(\overline{\mathcal{F}}\) is torsion-free over \(R\) (being a subsheaf of
724	  \(j_* \mathcal{F}_K\)), hence \(R\)-flat (flatness over a DVR
725	  \(=\) torsion-freeness), and restricts on the generic fiber to
726	  \(\mathcal{F}_K\). The pair \((\overline{\mathcal{F}}, \overline{q})\) is
727	  the unique extension of \(\langle \mathcal{F}_K, q_K\rangle\) to a
728	  \(\Spec R\)-point (uniqueness: any other extension agrees on the dense
729	  open \(X_K \subset X_R\) and is torsion-free, hence equals the image
730	  construction). The Hilbert polynomial is constant on connected
731	  \(\Spec R\) by flatness.
732	\end{proof}
733	
734	\section{Cohomology and base change}
735	\label{sec:cohomology_base_change}
736	
737	The Quot construction uses cohomology-and-base-change in two places: the
738	boundedness step (\cref{lem:quot_boundedness}) and the Grassmannian
739	embedding (\cref{lem:quot_alpha_injective}). We record the relevant
740	statement as a named theorem so the Lean encoding can cite it directly.
741	
742	\begin{theorem}\leanok
743	[Flat base change of cohomology]
744	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
745	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChangeCohomology}
746	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 02KH (lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology)
747	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947-L970).
748	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
749	  % "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
750	  %  \(\)
751	  %  \xymatrix{
752	  %  X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
753	  %  S' \ar[r]^g & S
754	  %  }
755	  %  \(\)
756	  %  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
757	  %  with pullback \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\).
758	  %  Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
759	  %  For any \(i \geq 0\)
760	  %  \begin{enumerate}
761	  %  \item the base change map of
762	  %  Cohomology, Lemma REF
763	  %  is an isomorphism
764	  %  \(\)
765	  %  g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
766	  %  \(\)
767	  %  \item if \(S = \Spec(A)\) and \(S' = \Spec(B)\), then
768	  %  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\).
769	  %  \end{enumerate}"
770	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 02KH (lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology).}
771	  Let
772	  \[
773	    \begin{array}{ccc}
774	      X' & \xrightarrow{g'} & X \\
775	      \downarrow f' &  & \downarrow f \\
776	      S' & \xrightarrow{g} & S
777	    \end{array}
778	  \]
779	  be a cartesian diagram of schemes with \(g\) flat and \(f\) quasi-compact and
780	  quasi-separated. Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent
781	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module with pull-back
782	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^* \mathcal{F}\). For every \(i \ge 0\):
783	  \begin{enumerate}
784	    \item the base-change map
785	      \(g^* R^i f_* \mathcal{F} \to R^i f'_* \mathcal{F}'\) is an isomorphism;
786	    \item if \(S = \Spec A\) and \(S' = \Spec B\), then $H^i(X, \mathcal{F})
787	      \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
788	  \end{enumerate}
789	\end{theorem}

791	\subsection{Project-side base-change substrate}
792	\label{subsec:quot_project_basechange}
793	
794	The Lean encoding of \cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology} (and the
795	Quot construction's iso between pushforward and tensor-pullback for the
796	universal section pattern) relies on four project-side declarations
797	introduced in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean}. These
798	are typed-sorry today: each carries a substantive Lean type that
799	encodes the intended mathematical statement, with the body deferred to
800	later iters (see \cref{sec:quot_lean_encoding} for the
801	phase-by-phase plan).
802	
803	\begin{definition}\leanok
804	[Canonical base-change map on sheaves of modules]
805	  \label{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map}
806	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.canonicalBaseChangeMap}
807	  \uses{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
808	  Given a cartesian square of schemes
809	  \(\square = (g' : X' \to X,\ f' : X' \to S',\ f : X \to S,\ g : S' \to S)\)
810	  with \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) and \(g\) flat, the
811	  \emph{canonical base-change map} on sheaves of modules is the natural
812	  transformation \(g'^* f^* \mathcal{F} \longrightarrow f'^* g^* \mathcal{F}\)
813	  arising from the universal property of pullback. Encoded as a morphism
814	  of \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\)-valued sheaf functors on the pulled-back open
815	  cover; this is the morphism whose iso-status discharges
816	  \cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}(1) in the present
817	  finite-locally-free / quasi-coherent-of-finite-type setting.
818	\end{definition}
819	
820	\begin{theorem}\leanok
821	[Stalkwise base-change isomorphism on affines]
822	  \label{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_app_app_isIso}
823	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.canonicalBaseChangeMap_app_app_isIso}
824	  \uses{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map, thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
825	  For every affine open \(V \subseteq S'\) and every affine open
826	  \(U \subseteq X\) lying over \(V\), the local component
827	  \((\text{canonicalBaseChangeMap}\ \square).\text{app}\ \mathcal{F}.\text{app}\ U\)
828	  of \cref{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map} is an isomorphism. Proof
829	  reduces (Stacks 02KH(2)) to the affine-base case: \(H^0\) commutes with
830	  flat extension of scalars on a quasi-compact quasi-separated affine
831	  morphism.
832	\end{theorem}
833	
834	\begin{theorem}\leanok
835	[Canonical base-change map is a sheaf isomorphism]
836	  \label{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_isIso}
837	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.canonicalBaseChangeMap_isIso}
838	  \uses{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_app_app_isIso}
839	  Globalising the stalk-wise statement
840	  \cref{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_app_app_isIso}: the natural
841	  transformation \(g'^* f^* \mathcal{F} \to f'^* g^* \mathcal{F}\) of
842	  \cref{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map} is an isomorphism of
843	  quasi-coherent sheaves of modules. This is the sheaf-level form of
844	  \cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}(1).
845	\end{theorem}

847	\begin{definition}\leanok

855	  \emph{tensor-presentation iso} packages the canonical iso

865	  (Step 2, project-side typed sorry): the

871	\begin{definition}

873	\leanok
874	[\(\Sigma\)-pair packaging: section-level LinearEquiv + intertwining]
875	  \label{def:pullback_app_isoTensor_sigma}
876	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap_sectionLinearEquiv}
877	  \uses{def:quot_pullback_app_isoTensor, def:quot_canonical_basechange_map,
878	        lem:pullback_tildeIso, lem:tildeIso_of_isQuasicoherent_isAffineOpen,
879	        lem:pullback_of_openImmersion_iso_restrict}
880	  Iter-188 Lane F-introduced \(\Sigma\)-pair packaging declaration that
881	  bundles BOTH the section-level LinearEquiv
882	  \(\Gamma(V,(g^*\mathcal N)) \;\simeq_{\Gamma(Y,U)}\; \Gamma(Y,U) \otimes
883	    \Gamma(N,V)\) AND its intertwining property with the canonical
884	  base-change map into a single \(\Sigma\)-struct returned via

889	                     \mathcal N) \;\simeq_{\Gamma(Y,U)}\;

891	      \forall x,\; f(1\otimes x)\;=\;\text{baseMap}\,g\,\mathcal N\,e\,x.

1067	  \(\top\)-section of \texttt{tilde} as the input module), then transport
1068	  the \(\Gamma(Y, U)\)-linear structure through the chain.
1069	
1070	  % NOTE (iter-189 plan-phase): body ~30-50 LOC, iter-190+ sub-build.
1071	  % This is Step 3 of the 3-pin unbundle of `_sectionLinearEquiv`, and
1072	  % is the LIGHTEST of the 3 pins per the analogist verdict (the chain
1073	  % `hU.toSpecΓ_fromSpec` + `tilde.isoTop` naturality is a routine but
1074	  % multi-step compositional transport). The iter-190 Lane F prover
1075	  % targets this pin axiom-clean.
1076	\end{proof}
1077	
1078	\section{Lean encoding}
1079	\label{sec:quot_lean_encoding}
1080	
1081	The Lean target file is the new
1082	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean}. The construction
1083	proceeds in three phases.
1084	
1085	\paragraph{Phase 1: Hilbert polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}).}
1086	On a finite-type morphism \(\pi : X \to S\) with \(S\) noetherian and a
1087	coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) with proper support over \(S\), the per-fiber
1088	Hilbert polynomial is encoded as a function $s \mapsto
1089	\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]$. Mathlib provides
1090	\texttt{CategoryTheory.PolynomialModule} and Euler-characteristic
1091	infrastructure on coherent sheaves on noetherian schemes; the polynomial
1092	property of \(m \mapsto \chi(\mathcal{F} \otimes L^{\otimes m})\) requires
1093	Snapper's Lemma, which is a project-side sub-build on top of the
1094	graded-Euler-characteristic infrastructure.
1095	
1096	\paragraph{Phase 2: Grassmannian scheme (\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme},
1097	\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).} The Grassmannian
1098	\(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) is encoded as the relative gluing of \(\binom{r}{d}\)
1099	affine charts \(U^I\) (each isomorphic to a relative affine space
1100	\(\mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_S\)) via the Pl\"ucker cocycle \(\theta_{I,J}\). The
1101	gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData}. The
1102	universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\) is glued by the transition cocycle
1103	\(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), and the determinant line bundle
1104	\(\det \mathcal{U}\) gives the Pl\"ucker closed embedding into
1105	\(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\). The representability statement uses
1106	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} (as in
1107	\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) on the universal pair
1108	\((\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d), \mathcal{U})\).
1109	
1110	\paragraph{Phase 3: Quot scheme (\cref{thm:quot_representable}).}
1111	The Quot functor is constructed as a locally closed subfunctor of the
1112	Grassmannian
1113	\(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\), via:
1114	\begin{enumerate}
1115	  \item the uniform \(m\)-regularity bound from
1116	    \cref{lem:quot_boundedness} (input: Castelnuovo--Mumford theorem,
1117	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.CastelnuovoMumford} -- project-side sub-build);
1118	  \item the Grassmannian embedding \(\alpha\) from
1119	    \cref{lem:quot_alpha_injective};
1120	  \item the flattening stratification (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
1121	    of \cref{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}, A.2.a) applied to the
1122	    universal quotient on the Grassmannian, producing the locally closed
1123	    stratum \(T_0^\Phi\);
1124	  \item the valuative-criterion upgrade
1125	    (\cref{lem:quot_valuative_criterion}) to closed embedding, hence
1126	    projective \(S\)-scheme.
1127	\end{enumerate}
1128	The reduction \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W} from the universal
1129	case to the general case is encoded as a closed embedding of representable
1130	subfunctors via the kernel-vanishing scheme.
1131	
1132	\paragraph{Cohomology and base change.}
1133	\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology} is the Mathlib-equivalent of
1134	Stacks tag 02KH. Mathlib carries a partial form
1135	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.flat\_base\_change}) for affine bases; the
1136	full \(R^i f_*\) statement is a project-side bridge if not present at the
1137	pinned commit.
1138	
1139	\paragraph{Dependencies summary.}
1140	This file directly consumes
1141	\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (A.1.a, on disk) for relative-spectrum
1142	gluing infrastructure and
1143	\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists} from
1144	\cref{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification} (A.2.a, written this iter)
1145	for the flattening stratum. Downstream, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}
1146	(A.2.c) consumes \cref{thm:quot_representable} via the special case
1147	\(E = \mathcal{O}_C\) on a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), giving the Hilbert
1148	scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot^{\Phi,\mathcal{O}_C(1)}_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
1149	and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective divisors.
1150	
1151	\section{Out of scope}
1152	\label{sec:quot_out_of_scope}
1153	
1154	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Quot-scheme existence statement and
1155	its proof skeleton via the Grassmannian + flattening-stratification
1156	route. The following are explicitly out of scope here:
1157	\begin{itemize}
1158	  \item \textbf{Flattening stratification} (A.2.a) -- consumed-by, not
1159	    proved-here. Its blueprint home is the new chapter
1160	    \texttt{Picard\_FlatteningStratification.tex}.
1161	  \item \textbf{Hilbert scheme as a separate chapter}. The Hilbert scheme
1162	    \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) is the special case
1163	    \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\) of the Quot scheme and is
1164	    introduced inline (\cref{def:quot_functor}); no separate chapter.
1165	  \item \textbf{Castelnuovo--Mumford \(m\)-regularity theorem} -- input to
1166	    \cref{lem:quot_boundedness}, not proved here. The proof lives in
1167	    Nitsure \S 2; for the Lean encoding it is a project-side sub-build.
1168	  \item \textbf{Semi-continuity and base change} (Nitsure \S 3) -- the
1169	    flat-base-change theorem of cohomology (\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology})
1170	    is stated here as a lemma, but the upstream Mathlib bridge / project-side
1171	    sub-build is not constructed in this chapter.
1172	  \item \textbf{Snapper's Lemma} -- the polynomial-eventually property of
1173	    Euler characteristics, used in \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Cited
1174	    via Nitsure \S 1 and (textbook reference) Hartshorne III.5.2;
1175	    project-side sub-build for the Lean encoding.
1176	  \item \textbf{Identification with \(\mathbb{P}^n\) and relative
1177	    Grassmannian variants} (Nitsure \S 1, Exercises (1)--(4)) --
1178	    optional applications, not consumed by Route A.
1179	  \item \textbf{Hironaka's counterexample to representability of
1180	    \(\Hilb_{X/\mathbb{C}}\) in the non-projective case} (Nitsure \S 1,
1181	    Exercise (8)) -- pedagogical only; Route A operates entirely in the
1182	    projective setting.
1183	  \item \textbf{Group-scheme structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)} (A.2.c) -- the
1184	    abelian-group-scheme refinement is downstream of the Quot existence
1185	    statement; its blueprint home is
1186	    \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
1187	  \item \textbf{Identity-component \(\Pic^0\) and abelian-variety
1188	    structure} (A.3) -- a separate Route A sub-row, fed by but not
1189	    constructed in this chapter.
1190	\end{itemize}

2	\label{chap:Genus0BaseObjects_Cross01Substrate}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/Cross01Substrate.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-189 plan-phase). This chapter is a project-bespoke
6	% substrate file landing two reusable lemmas needed to close the Lane B
7	% \(\mathbb{G}_m\)-scaling cocycle (path (III.c) of
8	% chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity) together with two off-target sorries
9	% (\texttt{gm\_geomIrred}, \texttt{projGm\_isReduced}) in
10	% \texttt{Genus0BaseObjects/GmScaling.lean}. Both substrates are
11	% generic in the geometric situation (the closed-immersion lift lemma is
12	% scheme-level; the tensor-domain lemma is ring-level keyed on the
13	% \texttt{projectiveLineBar} grading) and live in their own file rather
14	% than inline so they can be re-used by the three GmScaling
15	% applications and, if upstreamed, by Mathlib itself.
16	%
17	% The recipe consolidated below was produced by the
18	% \texttt{mathlib-analogist} subagent in iter-189 and is preserved

20	% ``Concrete project-side recipe''). The chapter itself is the

23	\section{Setup and motivation}
24	\label{sec:cross01_substrate_setup}
25	
26	The Lane B cocycle \texttt{gmScalingP1\_chart\_agreement\_cross01} reduces, after
27	the iter-188 (III.c) separated-locus structural setup, to a single substantive
28	factorisation: a pair morphism
29	\(s_{\text{pair}} : T \to T_0 \times_X T_1\) into a fibre product over the
30	projective line bar \(\mathrm{PLB}_{\overline{k}}\) must factor through the
31	diagonal closed immersion
32	\(\Delta : T_0 \times_{X} T_1 \hookrightarrow T_0 \times_{\overline{k}} T_1\)
33	of the separated-over-\(\overline{k}\) target. Concretely the missing primitive is
34	the equivalence
35	\[
36	  \exists\, h : Y \to Z,\ h \circ i = g \iff \mathrm{range}(g) \subseteq \mathrm{range}(i)
37	\]
38	for a closed immersion \(i\) with reduced source/target hypothesis, which Mathlib
39	ships only in the kernel-inequality form (\(\mathtt{IsClosedImmersion.lift}\),
40	keyed on \(f.\mathrm{ker} \le g.\mathrm{ker}\)). Substrate~1 fills this gap.
41	
42	The same Lane B cocycle, together with the two off-target instances
43	\texttt{gm\_geomIrred} and \texttt{projGm\_isReduced}, also requires
44	\[
45	  \mathrm{IsDomain}\bigl(\mathrm{Away}_{\overline{k}}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{GmRing}\,\overline{k}\bigr)
46	\]
47	for a homogeneous generator \(f\) of positive degree in the
48	\(\mathrm{projectiveLineBar}\) grading. Substrate~2 supplies this via a
49	canonical iso chain
50	\[
51	  \mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \overline{k}[t]
52	  \;\cong\; \mathrm{MvPolynomial}_{\mathrm{Unit}}(\mathrm{Away}\,f)
53	\]
54	followed by localisation at \(X()\), reducing the claim to the
55	already-formalised pattern from \texttt{projectiveLineBar\_isReduced}.
56	
57	\section{Closed-immersion lift by range inclusion}
58	\label{sec:lift_iff_range_subset}
59	
60	\begin{theorem}[lift through closed immersion via range containment]
61	  \label{thm:IsClosedImmersion_lift_iff_range_subset}
62	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsClosedImmersion.lift_iff_range_subset}
63	  Let \(X,Y,Z\) be schemes (Type \(u\)), let \(i : Z \to X\) be a closed
64	  immersion with \(Z\) reduced, and let \(g : Y \to X\) be a
65	  quasi-compact morphism with \(Y\) reduced. Then there exists a
66	  morphism \(h : Y \to Z\) with \(h \circ i = g\) if and only if the
67	  topological range of \(g\) is contained in the topological range of
68	  \(i\):
69	  \[
70	    (\exists\, h : Y \to Z,\ h \circ i = g)
71	    \;\iff\;
72	    \mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}}) \subseteq \mathrm{range}(i_{\text{base}}).
73	  \]
74	\end{theorem}
75	\begin{proof}
76	
77	  \emph{Forward direction} is range pushdown: if \(g = h \circ i\)
78	  then every point in \(\mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}})\) is the image
79	  under \(i_{\text{base}}\) of \(h_{\text{base}}(y)\) for the
80	  corresponding preimage \(y\).
81	
82	  \emph{Backward direction} threads the Galois connection between
83	  vanishing-ideal and support of an ideal sheaf. The proof has six
84	  steps; the Mathlib primitives cited are present at the project's
85	  pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}).
86	
87	  \begin{enumerate}
88	  \item \emph{Range of a closed immersion is its kernel support.}
89	    Because \(i\) is a closed immersion, \(i\) factors (up to
90	    isomorphism) as the canonical subscheme inclusion of its kernel
91	    ideal sheaf, so
92	    \(\mathrm{range}(i_{\text{base}}) = i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\).
93	    (Mathlib:
94	    \texttt{IdealSheafData.range\_subschemeι} +
95	    \texttt{Scheme.Hom.toImage}.)
96	  \item \emph{Range of \(g\) lies in \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\).}
97	    The unconditional inclusion
98	    \(\mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}}) \subseteq g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\)
99	    is shipped (\texttt{Scheme.Hom.range\_subset\_ker\_support}); under
100	    \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\;g]\), \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\)
101	    equals \(\overline{\mathrm{range}\,g_{\text{base}}}\)
102	    (\texttt{Scheme.Hom.support\_ker}). Since
103	    \(i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\) is closed and contains
104	    \(\mathrm{range}(g)\) by hypothesis, the closure also lies in
105	    \(i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\):
106	    \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support} \subseteq i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\).
107	    (Mathlib: \texttt{closure\_minimal}.)
108	  \item \emph{\(Y\) reduced \(\Rightarrow\) \(g.\mathrm{ker}\) is radical.}
109	    Under \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\;g]\),
110	    \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{ideal}\,U = \mathrm{ker}(g.\mathrm{app}\,U)\)
111	    (\texttt{Scheme.Hom.ker\_apply}). Reducedness of \(Y\) makes this
112	    ring kernel a radical ideal pointwise on each affine open, so
113	    \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{radical} = g.\mathrm{ker}\).
114	  \item \emph{\(Z\) reduced \(\Rightarrow\) \(i.\mathrm{ker}\) is
115	    radical.} Identical argument with \(i\) in place of \(g\)
116	    (closed immersions are quasi-compact, so the
117	    \texttt{ker\_apply} hypothesis is satisfied automatically).
118	  \item \emph{Galois-connection chain.} Combining Steps~2--4 with
119	    \texttt{IdealSheafData.vanishingIdeal\_support} (which equates
120	    the vanishing ideal of a support with the radical) gives
121	    \[
122	      i.\mathrm{ker}
123	        = i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{radical}
124	        = \mathrm{vanishingIdeal}(i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support})
125	        \le \mathrm{vanishingIdeal}(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support})
126	        = g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{radical}
127	        = g.\mathrm{ker}.
128	    \]
129	    The middle inequality uses the antitone half of the Galois
130	    connection (\texttt{IdealSheafData.gc}).
131	  \item \emph{Apply Mathlib's kernel-inequality lift.} The proof
132	    concludes by feeding \(\mathrm{hker} : i.\mathrm{ker} \le g.\mathrm{ker}\)
133	    to \texttt{IsClosedImmersion.lift} and reading off its
134	    factorisation lemma \texttt{lift\_fac}.
135	  \end{enumerate}
136	\end{proof}
137	
138	\section{Tensor reducedness over the algebraic closure}
139	\label{sec:tensor_AwayGm_isDomain}
140	
141	\begin{theorem}[domain structure on \(\mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{GmRing}\)]
142	  \label{thm:gmRing_tensor_homogeneousAway_isDomain}
143	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gmRing_tensor_homogeneousAway_isDomain}
144	  Let \(\overline{k}\) be a field, let \(d \ge 1\), and let \(f\) be a
145	  non-zero homogeneous polynomial of degree \(d\) in the
146	  \(\mathrm{projectiveLineBar}\) grading on
147	  \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}(\mathrm{Fin}\,2)\;\overline{k}\). Then the
148	  tensor product
149	  \[
150	    \mathrm{HomogeneousLocalization.Away}\bigl(\mathrm{projectiveLineBarGrading}\,\overline{k}\bigr)\,f
151	    \;\otimes_{\overline{k}}\;
152	    \mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}
153	  \]
154	  is a domain (and hence reduced).
155	\end{theorem}
156	\begin{proof}
157	  The proof exhibits a canonical isomorphism of \(\overline{k}\)-algebras
158	  identifying the tensor product with the localisation of a
159	  polynomial ring over a domain at a non-zero-divisor; both operations
160	  preserve the domain property.
161	
162	  \begin{enumerate}
163	  \item \emph{Domain instance on the homogeneous localisation.}
164	    \(\mathrm{Away}\,f := \mathrm{HomogeneousLocalization.Away}(\mathrm{projectiveLineBarGrading})\,f\)
165	    embeds via the \texttt{val\_injective} bridge into
166	    \(\mathrm{Localization.Away}\,f\); since the polynomial ring
167	    \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}(\mathrm{Fin}\,2)\;\overline{k}\) is a domain
168	    and \(f \ne 0\), the localisation is a domain, and the bridge
169	    transports the property to \(\mathrm{Away}\,f\). (Already used in
170	    \texttt{projectiveLineBar\_isReduced}; see
171	    \texttt{GmScaling.lean} L734--738 for the precise instance
172	    threading.)
173	  \item \emph{Collapse the tensor with the polynomial factor.}
174	    Recalling
175	    \(\mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}
176	       = \mathrm{Localization.Away}(X()\,:\,\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;\overline{k})\),
177	    apply \texttt{MvPolynomial.algebraTensorAlgEquiv} to get
178	    \[
179	      \mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;\overline{k}
180	      \;\cong\;
181	      \mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;(\mathrm{Away}\,f),
182	    \]
183	    a \(\overline{k}\)-algebra (in fact \(\mathrm{Away}\,f\)-algebra)
184	    isomorphism.
185	  \item \emph{Push the localisation through the tensor.}
186	    Tensoring with the localisation step
187	    \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;\overline{k} \to \mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}\)
188	    factors through Mathlib's tensor-base-change lemma
189	    \texttt{IsLocalization.Away.tensorRightEquiv}, yielding an
190	    isomorphism
191	    \[
192	      \mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}
193	      \;\cong\;
194	      \mathrm{Localization.Away}(\iota(X())\,:\,\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;(\mathrm{Away}\,f))
195	    \]
196	    where \(\iota\) is the canonical algebra map. The threading uses
197	    \texttt{IsScalarTower} compatibility and the
198	    \texttt{Algebra}/\(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\) instance on the
199	    tensored polynomial ring.
200	  \item \emph{Right-hand side is a domain.}
201	    \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;(\mathrm{Away}\,f)\) is a
202	    polynomial ring over a domain (Step~1), hence a domain by the
203	    standard instance. The generator \(X()\) is non-zero
204	    (\texttt{MvPolynomial.X\_ne\_zero}), so its powers form a
205	    submonoid of the non-zero-divisors
206	    (\texttt{powers\_le\_nonZeroDivisors\_of\_noZeroDivisors}); the
207	    localisation is then a domain by
208	    \texttt{IsLocalization.isDomain\_localization}.
209	  \end{enumerate}
210	
211	  Transferring the domain property along the iso of Step~3 closes the
212	  goal. Reducedness is a direct corollary of being a domain.
213	\end{proof}
214

1	\chapter{Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-\(0\) curve into an abelian variety are constant}
2	\label{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}
3	label{chap:avr_for_rr}
4	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/BareScheme.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/ChartIso.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/Points.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/GmScaling.lean AlgebraicJacobian/RigidityLemma.lean
5	% Local macro: diagrammatic (Lean `≫`) composition. Defined locally because this
6	% chapter is the writer's only editable file; flagged in the writer report for promotion
7	% to macros/common.tex if desired. \providecommand is a no-op if it is later added globally.
8	\providecommand{\fatsemi}{\mathbin{\,;\,}}
9	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157). This chapter is the dedicated home of the project's COMMITTED
10	% genus-0 route (route (c)): the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity stack. It is
11	% 1:1 with the new upstream Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, which
12	% imports AlgebraicJacobian.Genus and is imported by AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian (breaking the
13	% RigidityKbar -> Rigidity -> Jacobian import cycle so that genusZeroWitness can consume the
14	% keystone directly). The headline prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV is the char-free
15	% replacement (no [CharZero]) for the over-k-bar declaration rigidity_over_kbar of
16	% \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and
17	% still carries [CharZero]). The df=0 / H^0(P^1,Omega)=0 / Serre-duality framing is the
18	% fallback (a), NOT the route developed here.
19	The genus-\(0\) arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
20	(\(J = \Spec k\)); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
21	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus \(0\)
22	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
23	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field \(k\), so the
24	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
25	O(-2)) = 0\(, hence \)df = 0\(, hence \)f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
26	characteristic-\(p\) Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant \(f\) can have \(df = 0\). The
27	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
28	The chain has two parts. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford,
29	Form~I; equivalently Milne's Rigidity Theorem~1.1) is an elementary properness/closed-map
30	argument --- it uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, is the entry point
31	for formalisation, and is now proven axiom-clean. From the Rigidity Lemma, Milne's \S I.3
32	derives the genus-\(0\) base case \emph{without} the theorem of the cube --- and, in this
33	project's reading, \emph{without} Milne's Theorem~3.2 (rational-map extension), Lemma~3.3,
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials (the ``4th route''; see
35	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). The decisive observation is that \(\mathbb P^1\) is
36	\emph{already complete} and \(f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\) is \emph{already a total morphism}, so
37	there is no rational defect map to extend over a complete surface and Theorem~3.2 never enters.
38	Instead the base case is closed by the \textbf{\(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut} (the project's
39	primary route, an Archon-original realisation of Milne Proposition~3.9): one uses the
40	\emph{multiplicative scaling} action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to
41	\mathbb P^1\(, \)(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x\( (the M\"obius scaling fixing both \)0$ and
42	\(\infty\)), which is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb
43	G_m\( (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}). Normalising \)f(0) = \eta_A\( and composing \)h := \sigma_\times
44	\fatsemi f\(, one applies additivity of \)\mathrm{Hom}(-, A)$ over a product
45	(\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires
46	only the \emph{first} factor complete --- with \(V = \mathbb P^1\) proper, \(W = \mathbb G_m\), and
47	base points \(0 \in \mathbb P^1\), \(1 \in \mathbb G_m\). Because \(0\) is a \emph{fixed point} of
48	scaling, the \(W\)-axis restriction \(\lambda \mapsto h(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0)\) is
49	\emph{constant} at \(\eta_A\), so the decomposition collapses to \(h = p \fatsemi f\), i.e.\
50	\(f(\lambda x) = f(x)\). Specialising \(x = 1\) gives that \(f|_{\mathbb G_m}\) is constant, and since
51	\(\mathbb G_m\) is dense in \(\mathbb P^1\) with \(A\) separated, \(f\) is constant
52	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, via the separated-target/dominant-source handle
53	\cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}). This route uses \emph{only} the proven Corollary~1.5, a
54	scaling fixed point, and density --- it never forms the additive defect map, never invokes
55	\(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0\), and so sidesteps the ``image of a group homomorphism is a
56	closed subgroup'' input that has no Mathlib support. The classical \(\mathbb G_a\)-additive
57	argument (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial} via the \(\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m\) incompatibility
58	\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, the heart of Milne Proposition~3.9) is retained only as the
59	Milne-faithful alternative and is \emph{off} the genus-\(0\) critical path. The corollary that a
60	pointed regular map of abelian
61	varieties is a homomorphism (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}, Milne Corollary~1.2) is recorded
62	for completeness and for Route~A but is \emph{not} needed on the genus-\(0\) path; Milne's
63	Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is likewise demoted to a Route-A-only item
64	(the Albanese universal property), no longer on the genus-\(0\) critical path
65	(\cref{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}). Combining these
66	gives that every morphism \(\mathbb P^1 \to A\) is constant
67	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing with the classification of genus-\(0\)
68	curves (\cref{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}, \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\) over \(\bar k\)) yields the
69	headline \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. The theorem of the cube (Milne \S I.5) is
70	\emph{not} used anywhere on this path (see \cref{rmk:cube_not_needed}); the chain invokes
71	neither Serre duality nor the FGA \(\Pic\) representability engine, so the genus-\(0\) arm is
72	fully decoupled from Route~A.
73	\medskip
74	Throughout, \(\bar k\) is an algebraically closed field of arbitrary characteristic. An
75	\emph{abelian variety} over \(\bar k\) is a complete (proper) irreducible group variety; in the
76	project's Lean encoding \(A\) is an object of \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) carrying
77	\(\mathtt{GrpObj}\), \(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{Smooth}\), and \(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\).
78	\section{The Rigidity Lemma}
79	\begin{lemma}
80	\leanok
81	[Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I)]
82	  \label{thm:rigidity_lemma}
83	  \uses{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
84	  
85	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_lemma}
86	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let \(X\) be a complete variety, \(Y\) and \(Z\)
88	  % any varieties, and \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) a morphism such that for some
89	  % \(y_0 \in Y\), \(f(X \times \{y_0\})\) is a single point \(z_0\) of \(Z\). Then there is a
90	  % morphism \(g \colon Y \to Z\) such that if \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is the
91	  % projection, \(f = g \circ p_2\)."
92	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43.}
93	  Let \(X\) be a complete variety, and let \(Y\), \(Z\) be any varieties over \(\bar k\). Let
94	  \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism such that for some \(y_0 \in Y\) the image
95	  \(f(X \times \{y_0\})\) is a single point \(z_0 \in Z\). Then there is a morphism
96	  \(g \colon Y \to Z\) with \(f = g \circ p_2\), where \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is the
97	  projection; that is, \(f\) depends only on the \(Y\)-coordinate, and \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\) for any
98	  fixed \(x_0 \in X\).
99	\end{lemma}
100	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point \(x_0 \in X\), and define \(g \colon Y \to Z\) by
101	% \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\). Since \(X \times Y\) is a variety, to show that \(f = g \circ p_2\), it is
102	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of \(X \times Y\). Let
103	% \(U\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), \(F = Z - U\), and \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\);
104	% then \(G\) is closed in \(Y\) since \(X\) is complete and hence \(p_2\) is a closed map. Further
105	% \(y_0 \notin G\) since \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}\). Therefore \(Y - G = V\) is a non-empty
106	% open subset of \(Y\). For each \(y \in V\), the complete variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by
107	% \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single point of \(U\). But this means that for
108	% any \(x \in X\), \(y \in V\), \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)\), and this proves our
109	% assertion."
110	\begin{proof}
111	\leanok
112	% NOTE (iter-159 review): the iter-157 unsoundness REMAINS REPAIRED. The collapse hypothesis
113	% (_hf : f(X x {y0}) = {z0}) is threaded through all three helpers (rigidity_lemma ->
114	% rigidity_core -> rigidity_eqOn_dense_open) and GENUINELY CONSUMED in the body of
115	% rigidity_eqOn_dense_open (proves y0 \notin G, the load-bearing non-emptiness of V := Y - G).
116	% The counterexample f = fst is excluded (under f = fst, _hf forces X to collapse to a point).
117	% rigidity_eqOn_dense_open is sorry-free in its OWN body (hfib, the pullback-fibre fact over the
118	% k-bar-point y0, is closed). rigidity_snd_lift and snd_left_isClosedMap are axiom-clean (no sorryAx).
119	% NOTE (iter-160): the \uses edges run FORWARD along the true Lean dependency order
120	% (rigidity_lemma -> rigidity_eqOn_dense_open -> rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine), so the
121	% not-proven status of the residual sorry propagates up and de-launders BOTH thm:rigidity_lemma and
122	% lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open. No backward edge from the leaf lemmas back up the chain.
123	% NOTE (iter-161): bridge 2 (rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine) was DECOMPOSED into two named
124	% top-level sub-lemmas along the route-B (cohomology-free) split:
125	%   - Step 2, morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints: "two morphisms agreeing at every closed point of a
126	%     reduced Jacobson source into a separated target are equal" -- PROVEN axiom-clean (the
127	%     dense-closed-points coproduct probe + ext_of_isDominant; the connective Mathlib lacks).
128	%   - Step 1, rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine: Mumford's per-closed-slice constancy
129	%     -- the genuinely-deep residual, still `sorry`. THIS is now the chain's lone deep residual
130	%     (bridge 2's body is real assembly: Step 2 proven, the JacobsonSpace U instance now derivable).
131	% SIGNATURE CHANGE (iter-161, plan-authorized): Step 2's globalisation needs the closed points of
132	% the saturated open U to be DENSE, i.e. U Jacobson, which over Spec k-bar follows from (X tensor Y)
133	% being LOCALLY OF FINITE TYPE. So [LocallyOfFiniteType (X tensor Y).hom] is now carried on FIVE
134	% chain lemmas (rigidity_lemma, rigidity_core, rigidity_eqOn_dense_open,
135	% rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine, rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine), in
136	% addition to [IsAlgClosed kbar]. Free downstream: curves and abelian varieties are of finite type
137	% over k-bar. The earlier "[IsAlgClosed] is the only added instance" claim is hereby retired.
138	  Fix any point \(x_0 \in X\) and define \(g \colon Y \to Z\) by \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\) (the
139	  composite of \(y \mapsto (x_0, y)\) with \(f\)). Since \(X \times Y\) is a variety (in
140	  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
141	  the equality of morphisms \(f = g \circ p_2\) it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
142	  open subset of \(X \times Y\).
143	  Let \(U\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), put \(F = Z - U\) (a closed set),
144	  and set \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\). Because \(X\) is complete, the projection
145	  \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
146	  completeness/properness); hence \(G\), the image of the closed set \(f^{-1}(F)\), is closed in
147	  \(Y\). The hypothesis \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U\) gives
148	  \(f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing\), so \(y_0 \notin G\). Therefore
149	  \(V := Y - G\) is a non-empty open subset of \(Y\).
150	  For each \(y \in V\) we have \(X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing\), i.e.
151	  \(f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U\). Thus the complete (proper) variety \(X \times \{y\}\) is
152	  mapped by \(f\) into the \emph{affine} variety \(U\). A proper connected variety mapping to an
153	  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
154	  variety is constant). Hence \(f(X \times \{y\})\) is one point, necessarily
155	  \(f(x_0, y) = g(y)\). So for all \(x \in X\) and \(y \in V\),
156	  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
157	  The two morphisms \(f\) and \(g \circ p_2\) thus agree on the non-empty open
158	  \(X \times V\), which is dense in the irreducible \(X \times Y\); as \(Z\) is separated they
159	  agree everywhere. \qedhere
160	\end{proof}
161	\begin{remark}
162	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_cube_free}
163	  The proof of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} uses \emph{only} (i) that completeness of \(X\) makes
164	  \(p_2\) a closed map, and (ii) that a proper connected variety has no nonconstant map to an
165	  affine variety. It uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is valid in
166	  arbitrary characteristic. This is the designated formalisation entry point: it is the
167	  lowest-prerequisite link of the chain. (Mumford gives this as the geometric heart of the
168	  ``second proof'' of commutativity of an abelian variety; Milne packages the same statement
169	  as the Rigidity Theorem~1.1, requiring \(V\) complete and \(V \times W\) geometrically
170	  irreducible.)
171	\end{remark}
172	\begin{remark}
173	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_decomposition}
174	  The Lean formalisation of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} splits into three helper steps, mirroring
175	  the proof above: \texttt{rigidity\_snd\_lift} (the cartesian-monoidal algebra identifying the
176	  collapsed map \((x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)\) followed by \(f\) with \(g \circ p_2\));
177	  \texttt{rigidity\_core} (the scheme-level gluing: two maps out of the geometrically irreducible
178	  reduced \(X \times Y\) into the separated \(Z\) that agree on a non-empty open subset agree
179	  everywhere, by separatedness); and \texttt{rigidity\_eqOn\_dense\_open}
180	  (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}, the genuine geometry: the construction of the non-empty
181	  open \(X \times V\) and the slice-constancy of \(f\) on it). The collapse hypothesis
182	  \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}\) must be threaded through \emph{all three} helpers, since it is
183	  what makes \(V\) non-empty; a decomposition that drops it from the lower helpers is unsound
184	  (see \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}).

186	  helper \texttt{snd\_left\_isClosedMap}. The fibre-over-\(y_0\) fact is \textbf{closed} (via the

190	  cohomology-free route-(c) split into two named sub-lemmas: the globalisation
191	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} (Step~2, ``agree at every closed point of a reduced
192	  Jacobson source \(\Rightarrow\) equal'') is \textbf{proven axiom-clean}, and the per-closed-slice
193	  constancy \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine} (Step~1, Mumford's slice

195	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} is therefore real assembly (Step~2 over Step~1).

197	  % Rigidity-Lemma chain (thm:rigidity_lemma … lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine,
198	  % via the new helper lem:isIntegral_of_retract_of_integral) is sorry-free + axiom-clean

216	\begin{lemma}
217	\leanok
218	[Dense-open agreement: the geometric heart of the Rigidity Lemma]
219	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
220	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
221	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
222	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let \(X\) be a complete variety, \(Y\) and \(Z\)
223	  % any varieties, and \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) a morphism such that for some
224	  % \(y_0 \in Y\), \(f(X \times \{y_0\})\) is a single point \(z_0\) of \(Z\). Then there is a
225	  % morphism \(g \colon Y \to Z\) such that if \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is the
226	  % projection, \(f = g \circ p_2\)."
227	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
228	  dense-open construction is the body of that proof).}
229	  Let \(X\) be a complete variety and \(Y\), \(Z\) any varieties over \(\bar k\). Let
230	  \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism, fix a point \(x_0 \in X\), and suppose given
231	  \(y_0 \in Y\), \(z_0 \in Z\) with the \emph{collapse hypothesis}
232	  \[ f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \qquad
233	     \text{(in Lean: } \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathbf 1_X)\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi y_0) \fatsemi f
234	     = \mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi z_0\text{).} \]
235	  Then there is a non-empty open subset \(U \subseteq X \times Y\) on which \(f\) agrees, as a
236	  scheme morphism, with the collapsed map \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\), where
237	  $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,
238	  (p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y)\( is the ``\)(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$'' endomorphism. Concretely
239	  \(U = X \times V\) with \(V := Y - G\), \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(Z - U_0))\) for an affine open
240	  neighbourhood \(U_0\) of \(z_0\), and on \(U\) one has \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y)\).
241	  \medskip
242	  \noindent\textbf{The collapse hypothesis is load-bearing.} The non-emptiness of \(V\) is exactly
243	  the assertion \(y_0 \notin G\), and this holds \emph{because} $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}
244	  \subseteq U_0\( keeps the slice over \)y_0\( inside the affine \)U_0\(, away from \)F = Z - U_0$. A
245	  version of this lemma \emph{without} the collapse hypothesis is false: for \(X = Y = Z\) and
246	  \(f = p_1\) the first projection, \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f \colon (x, y) \mapsto x_0\)
247	  agree on no non-empty open (the agreement locus is contained in \(\{x = x_0\}\), which has empty
248	  interior in the irreducible \(X \times Y\)). Therefore the formal target \emph{must} carry the
249	  collapse hypothesis as an explicit antecedent; dropping it (as an earlier decomposition
250	  silently did) makes the deferred goal unsatisfiable.
251	  \medskip
252	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization note: algebraically closed base, and locally-of-finite-type
253	  source.} The prose already takes \(\bar k\) algebraically closed, but the formal statement must
254	  \emph{encode} two properties. First, the cohomology-free proof of the slice-constancy bridge
255	  (below) needs each per-closed-point residue field \(\kappa(y)\) to be \(\bar k\) itself, so that a
256	  proper integral slice has global sections \(\Gamma = \bar k\) and maps to a single \(\bar k\)-point of
257	  the affine \(U_0\); this requires \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\). Second, the globalisation of the
258	  per-slice agreement (Step~2 of the bridge, \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}) needs the
259	  closed points of the saturated open \(U\) to be \emph{dense} --- i.e.\ \(U\) to be a Jacobson space.
260	  Over the algebraically closed \(\bar k\) this holds precisely because \(X \times Y\) is \emph{locally of
261	  finite type} over \(\bar k\) (\texttt{LocallyOfFiniteType.jacobsonSpace} transports the Jacobson
262	  property of \(\Spec \bar k\), and \texttt{closure\_closedPoints} then gives density); this requires
263	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\). Accordingly the formalization carries
264	  \emph{both} \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) and
265	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\) on this lemma (in addition to
266	  \([\mathtt{Field}\ \bar k]\)), and propagates the same two instances to \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
267	  its core helper \texttt{rigidity\_core}, and the bridge sub-lemmas
268	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} and
269	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}. Both cost nothing downstream: the
270	  consumers \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} and \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} (Lean
271	  \texttt{rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme}) \emph{already} assume
272	  \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) --- the base-field variable is literally named \(\mathtt{kbar}\) ---
273	  and curves and abelian varieties are of finite type over \(\bar k\), so the
274	  locally-of-finite-type instance is automatic there.
275	\end{lemma}
276	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point \(x_0 \in X\), and define \(g \colon Y \to Z\) by
277	% \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\). Since \(X \times Y\) is a variety, to show that \(f = g \circ p_2\), it is
278	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of \(X \times Y\). Let
279	% \(U\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), \(F = Z - U\), and \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\);
280	% then \(G\) is closed in \(Y\) since \(X\) is complete and hence \(p_2\) is a closed map. Further
281	% \(y_0 \notin G\) since \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}\). Therefore \(Y - G = V\) is a non-empty
282	% open subset of \(Y\). For each \(y \in V\), the complete variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by
283	% \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single point of \(U\). But this means that for
284	% any \(x \in X\), \(y \in V\), \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)\), and this proves our
285	% assertion."
286	\begin{proof}
287	  \leanok
288	  Let \(U_0\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), put \(F = Z - U_0\), and set
289	  \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\). Because \(X\) is complete, the projection \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is a
290	  \emph{closed} map, so \(G\) (the image of the closed set \(f^{-1}(F)\)) is closed in \(Y\). The
291	  collapse hypothesis \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U_0\) gives
292	  \(f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing\), hence \(y_0 \notin G\); this is the only place
293	  the collapse hypothesis is used, and it is essential, for \(V := Y - G\) is non-empty precisely
294	  because \(y_0 \in V\). For each \(y \in V\) the proper variety \(X \times \{y\}\) is mapped by \(f\)
295	  into the affine \(U_0\), hence to a single point, necessarily \(f(x_0, y)\). So for all \(x \in X\),
296	  \(y \in V\), we have \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x, y)\), i.e.\ \(f\) and
297	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree on the non-empty open \(U := X \times V\). (In the Lean
298	  encoding the two slice-constancy bridges are the closed-map step from properness and the
299	  ``proper-into-affine has single-point image'' step; see the docstring of
300	  \texttt{rigidity\_core}.)
301	  \medskip
302	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization notes (the concrete char-free Mathlib route).} The textbook
303	  proof above is realised in Lean by three pieces. They cite Mathlib lemma names as the chosen
304	  route; none uses cohomology, and the relative Stein-factorisation framing is deliberately
305	  \emph{avoided} (see below).
306	  \emph{Bridge~1 (the closed-map step) --- BUILT.} ``\(X\) complete \(\Rightarrow p_2\) closed'' is
307	  the axiom-clean helper \texttt{snd\_left\_isClosedMap} (iter-158). It is obtained from
308	  \texttt{IsProper.toUniversallyClosed} on \(X\), base-changed across the canonical pullback square

377	  Let \(\bar k\) be algebraically closed and let \(X\), \(Y\), \(Z\) be objects of
378	  \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) with \(X\) proper, \(X \times Y\) geometrically irreducible, reduced,
379	  and \emph{locally of finite type} over \(\bar k\) (the formalization carries
380	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\), which makes the saturated open \(U\)
381	  below a Jacobson space, so its closed points are dense --- the hypothesis Step~2 of the proof

383	  \(x_0 \colon \mathbf 1 \to X\), and write $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,
384	  (\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,(p_2)\( for the ``\)(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$''
385	  endomorphism. Suppose:

389	      its points);

399	  variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single
400	  point of \(U\)''. We realise it by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route that is

407	  Fix a closed point \(x\) of \(U\), lying over a closed point \(y = p_2(x) \in V_{\mathrm{set}}\).

410	  \emph{entire} fibre \(X_y \cong X\) over \(y\); hence \(f\) maps the proper integral slice \(X_y\) into the
411	  affine \(U_0\). By \(\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}\) the global sections \(\Gamma(X_y)\) form a
412	  field, module-finite over \(\bar k\) by \(\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}\), and

414	  the affine \(U_0\) is pinned by its ring map on global sections (\(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\)), so the
415	  restriction of \(f\) to \(X_y\) factors through a single \(\bar k\)-point of \(U_0\) --- necessarily the
416	  point \(f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x)\). Thus \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\)
417	  agree (after the residue-field probe) at every closed point of \(U\). This per-slice statement is the
418	  chain's single genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.

420	  \noindent\textbf{Step 2 (globalisation over dense closed points) --- \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, proven.}
421	  The closed points are dense in the locally-of-finite-type \(\bar k\)-scheme \(U\)
422	  (\(\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}\) / the Jacobson-space property, supplied here by the new hypothesis
423	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\)). To turn ``\(f\) and
424	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree at each closed point'' into a single morphism equality on \(U\),
425	  the proven globalisation \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} assembles the closed points

428	  it to the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity handle \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) (the same
429	  family \(\mathtt{rigidity\_core}\) uses). This yields the morphism equality
430	  \(U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)\) on all of \(U\). The
431	  ``agrees on a dense set of closed points \(\Rightarrow\) hom-equality'' connective is the one piece
432	  Mathlib does not package directly (it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
433	  \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\)); that bespoke build is now done in
434	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, and it is \emph{not} cohomology. \qedhere

436	\begin{lemma}\leanok
437	[Step~2: morphisms agreeing at every closed point of a Jacobson source are equal]
438	  \label{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}

441	  % Project-bespoke assembly: NO external source. Mathlib supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
442	  % ext_of_isDominant; the "dense closed points => hom-extensionality" connective is built here.
443	  Let \(W\), \(Z\) be schemes with \(W\) \emph{reduced}, \(W\) a \emph{Jacobson space}, and \(Z\)
444	  \emph{separated}. Let \(g_1, g_2 \colon W \to Z\) be morphisms. If, for every \emph{closed} point
445	  \(x \in W\), the two morphisms agree after restriction along the residue-field point
446	  \(\Spec \kappa(x) \to W\) (in Lean, $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 =
447	  W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2\(), then \)g_1 = g_2$.
448	\end{lemma}
449	\begin{proof}
450	\leanok
451	  This is the ``dense closed points \(\Rightarrow\) hom-extensionality'' connective that Mathlib does
452	  not package directly: it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism extensionality
453	  \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) (a dominant morphism into a separated target is an epimorphism for
454	  the purpose of right-cancellation). We assemble the per-closed-point hypotheses into one such
455	  dominant probe.
456	  Form the coproduct \(P := \coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} \Spec \kappa(x)\) and the morphism
457	  \(\mathrm{probe} \colon P \to W\) defined by \(\mathtt{Sigma.desc}\) of the residue-field points
458	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\). Its topological range contains every closed point of \(W\):
459	  the inclusion \(\iota_x \colon \Spec \kappa(x) \to P\) composes with \(\mathrm{probe}\) to
460	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\) (the defining property of \(\mathtt{Sigma.desc}\)), and the
461	  range of \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\) is the singleton \(\{x\}\)
462	  (\(\mathtt{Scheme.range\_fromSpecResidueField}\)). Because \(W\) is a Jacobson space, the closed points
463	  are dense (\(\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}\), i.e.\ \(\overline{\mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} = W\)), so
464	  the range of \(\mathrm{probe}\) is dense and \(\mathrm{probe}\) is \emph{dominant}.
465	  Componentwise, \(g_1\) and \(g_2\) agree after \(\mathrm{probe}\): precomposing with each coproduct
466	  inclusion \(\iota_x\) reduces (by \(\mathtt{Sigma.\iota\_desc}\)) to
467	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 = W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2\),
468	  which is the hypothesis at the closed point \(x\). By the universal property of the coproduct
469	  (\(\mathtt{Sigma.hom\_ext}\)) this gives \(\mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_1 = \mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_2\).
470	  Since \(\mathrm{probe}\) is dominant and \(Z\) is separated, \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) cancels it
471	  on the left to yield \(g_1 = g_2\).
472	\end{proof}
