1	\chapter{Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form; Mumford \S4 in the abelian-variety case)}
2	\label{chap:Rigidity}
3	
4	This chapter records a self-contained helper: \emph{rigidity for morphisms of schemes that agree on a non-empty open}. The classical motivation is Mumford's rigidity for abelian-variety morphisms, but the project's formalised statement is more general: the source-side group-object hypothesis is unused by the proof and is dropped.
5	
6	The lemma is independent of the construction of \(\Jac(C)\). It is the load-bearing input for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-0 Albanese-witness argument (Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b), where the source is taken to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (which is \emph{not} a group scheme).
7	
8	\section{Statement}
9	
10	\begin{theorem}\leanok
11	[Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form)]
12	  \label{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen}
14	  Let \(k\) be a field. Let \(X\) be a reduced geometrically irreducible \(k\)-scheme and let \(Y\) be a separated \(k\)-scheme. Let \(g_1, g_2 \colon X \to Y\) be two morphisms of \(k\)-schemes, and let \(U \subseteq X\) be a non-empty open subset. If
15	  \[
16	    U.\iota \circ g_1 \;=\; U.\iota \circ g_2
17	  \]
18	  as scheme morphisms (where \(U.\iota \colon U \hookrightarrow X\) is the canonical open immersion), then \(g_1 = g_2\) as morphisms of \(k\)-schemes.
19	\end{theorem}
20	
21	\begin{remark}
22	  The classical Mumford rigidity theorem (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S4) usually states the special case where \(Y\) is an abelian variety and the conclusion is phrased as ``a morphism vanishing on a non-empty open is zero''. The two forms are interderivable in the abelian-variety case via the difference morphism \(\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}\) formed from the group law on \(Y\). The project formalises the more general statement above because the M2.a (genus-0 Albanese-witness) consumer specifically needs the source to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\), which is not a group scheme: the abelian-variety packaging is consumer-side and the scheme-level statement is what the proof actually establishes.
23	
24	  The refactor that landed the scheme-level form (iter-125; see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md}) dropped 8 unused hypotheses from the project's original group-scheme-flavoured declaration and weakened the target-side \([\mathtt{IsProper}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) to \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\).
25	\end{remark}
26	
27	\section{Proof sketch}
28	
29	\begin{proof}
30	  \leanok
31	  We reduce to Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}), which gives the standard scheme-level fact that two morphisms from a reduced scheme to a separated scheme agreeing on a dominant subscheme agree everywhere.
32	
33	  The three load-bearing instances feeding that closure are:
34	  \begin{itemize}
35	    \item \emph{\(Y \to \Spec k\) is separated.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) provides this directly (no projection from \(\mathtt{IsProper}\) needed). The \texttt{OverClass.fromOver} instance transports it to the form \(\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ (Y.\mathrm{left} \downarrow \Spec k)\) that the closure step expects.
36	    \item \emph{\(X\) is an irreducible space.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\ X.\mathrm{hom}]\) over the field-spectrum base \(\Spec k\) (which is a single point, hence subsingleton) yields \(\mathtt{IrreducibleSpace}\ X.\mathrm{left}\) via \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton}.
37	    \item \emph{The inclusion \(U.\iota\) is dominant.} A non-empty open in an irreducible space is dense, and a dense open immersion is dominant: \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι}.
38	  \end{itemize}
39	  With these three derived instances and the hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsReduced}\ X.\mathrm{left}]\), the closure \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} produces the underlying scheme-morphism equality \(g_1.\mathrm{left} = g_2.\mathrm{left}\) from the agreement-on-\(U\) hypothesis. The promotion to \(g_1 = g_2\) in \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\) uses \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext}.
40	
41	  \medskip
42	  \noindent\textbf{Mathlib ingredients.}
43	  \begin{itemize}
44	    \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} for the scheme-level closure step.
45	    \item \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton} to derive irreducibility of \(X.\mathrm{left}\).
46	    \item \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι} to derive dominance of the open immersion \(U.\iota\) from the non-empty hypothesis.
47	    \item \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext} to promote underlying scheme equality to \(\mathrm{Over}\)-morphism equality.
48	    \item No abelian-variety / group-object infrastructure is needed: smoothness, properness, and group-object hypotheses on \(X\) and \(Y\) are all dropped from the formalised statement.
49	  \end{itemize}
50	\end{proof}
51	
52	\section{Use in the project}
53	
54	The rigidity theorem is used in two places downstream:
55	
56	\begin{itemize}
57	  \item \textbf{M2.a (genus-0 Albanese witness).} Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b reduces the genus-0 rigidity-over-\(\bar k\) argument (for \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}\) hitting the identity at a \(\bar k\)-point) to this theorem with \(X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(Y := A_{\bar k}\). The refactor of iter-125 (dropping the source-side group-object hypothesis) is what enables this application. The named project declaration packaging the C.2.a–C.2.e reduction is \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} in \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}; that chapter scaffolds the iter-126 Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} with the C.2.d cotangent-vanishing keystone residual.
58	  \item \textbf{Uniqueness in the Albanese property.} Used in the uniqueness half of Theorem~REF (the Albanese property of \(\Jac(C)\)): given two morphisms \(g_1, g_2 \colon \Jac(C) \to A\) with \(g_1 \circ \alpha_P = g_2 \circ \alpha_P\), scheme-level agreement on the image of \(\alpha_P\) (a non-empty open after passing to \(C^{(g)} \twoheadrightarrow \Jac(C)\)) forces \(g_1 = g_2\).
59	\end{itemize}
60	
61	Note: the existence half of REF requires Phase~B/C infrastructure (Picard scheme, dual abelian variety). Rigidity alone yields \emph{uniqueness} only.
62	
63	\section{Mathlib status}
64	
65	The scheme-level form is a thin specialization of Mathlib's existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}); the project's contribution is the \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\)-bundled packaging plus the convenience derivations of \texttt{IrreducibleSpace} and \texttt{IsDominant} from the project-side hypotheses.
66	
67	An upstream-PR candidate is a thin corollary in Mathlib's \texttt{Separated} file:
68	\[
69	  \texttt{ext\_of\_eqOnNonemptyOpen} \;\colon\; \texttt{(non-empty open) + (reduced source) + (separated target) }\Rightarrow\texttt{ ext}
70	\]
71	packaging the dense-and-dominant chain into a single API call. The incremental value over the existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} is small (just the API convenience of "non-empty open" over "dominant morphism"); see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md} for the proposed statement and home file. The project does not pursue this upstream PR as part of the iter-125 work.

1445	  orthogonal strategies have been considered for closing this lemma. As of iter-187 the

1510	      The chart-agreement equation
1511	      \(\mathrm{pullback.fst} \fatsemi \mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\,\bar k\,0 =
1512	      \mathrm{pullback.snd} \fatsemi \mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\,\bar k\,1\)
1513	      on the cover intersection
1514	      \(\mathrm{pullback}\,((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_0.f)\,
1515	      ((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_1.f)\) is the statement that two morphisms
1516	      \(\mathrm{chart}_0, \mathrm{chart}_1 \colon \mathrm{(intersection)} \to
1517	      \overline{\mathbb P^1}\) agree on that intersection scheme. \emph{The key
1518	      observation is that \(\overline{\mathbb P^1}\) is separated over \(\bar k\)},
1519	      so the diagonal
1520	      \(\Delta_{\overline{\mathbb P^1}} \colon
1521	        \overline{\mathbb P^1} \longrightarrow
1522	        \overline{\mathbb P^1} \times_{\bar k} \overline{\mathbb P^1}\)
1523	      is a closed immersion. Because \(\overline{\mathbb P^1} = \Proj(\bar k[X_0, X_1])\)
1524	      is the \(\Proj\) of a graded \(\bar k\)-algebra, its separatedness over \(\Spec
1525	      \bar k\) is the standard Stacks fact ``\(\Proj(S)\) is separated'' (Stacks tag
1526	      \texttt{01KU} / lemma \texttt{lemma-proj-separated}); Mathlib supplies the
1527	      categorical packaging via \(\mathtt{IsSeparated}\) and
1528	      \(\mathtt{IsSeparated.diagonal\_isClosedImmersion}\).
1529	      \emph{Bypass.} Reformulate the cocycle as: the pair-morphism
1530	      \((\mathrm{chart}_0, \mathrm{chart}_1) \colon \mathrm{(intersection)}
1531	      \longrightarrow \overline{\mathbb P^1} \times_{\bar k} \overline{\mathbb P^1}\)
1532	      (built via \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Limits.prod.lift}\) on
1533	      \(\mathrm{chart}_0 := \mathrm{pullback.fst} \fatsemi \mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\,
1534	      \bar k\,0\) and
1535	      \(\mathrm{chart}_1 := \mathrm{pullback.snd} \fatsemi \mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\,
1536	      \bar k\,1\)) \emph{factors through the diagonal
1537	      \(\Delta_{\overline{\mathbb P^1}}\)}. By Mathlib's
1538	      \(\mathtt{IsClosedImmersion.lift\_iff\_range\_subset}\) (the universal property
1539	      of a closed immersion: a morphism factors through a closed immersion iff its
1540	      set-theoretic image is contained in the image of the immersion), the
1541	      factorization is equivalent to the set-level containment of the cocycle's image
1542	      in the image of \(\Delta\), which on points is exactly the chart equality on
1543	      the \emph{closed points} of the intersection. The closed-point check is
1544	      discrete (each closed point of the intersection lies over a single \(\bar k\)-
1545	      point of \(D_+(X_0 \cdot X_1) \times \mathbb G_m\) and the two chart maps both
1546	      evaluate to the same \(\bar k\)-point of \(\overline{\mathbb P^1}\)
1547	      corresponding to that \((x, \lambda)\)), and bypasses the simp coverage gap
1548	      that obstructs (III.a)/(III.b) entirely.

1553	            \longrightarrow \overline{\mathbb P^1} \times_{\bar k}
1554	            \overline{\mathbb P^1}\)
1555	          via Mathlib's \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Limits.prod.lift}\,(\mathbf
1556	          1_{\overline{\mathbb P^1}})\,(\mathbf 1_{\overline{\mathbb P^1}})\), and
1557	          obtain the closed-immersion witness from
1558	          \(\mathtt{IsSeparated.diagonal\_isClosedImmersion}\) applied to the
1559	          separated structure morphism
1560	          \(\overline{\mathbb P^1}.\mathrm{hom} \colon \overline{\mathbb P^1} \to
1561	          \Spec \bar k\).
1562	        \item Build the pair-morphism
1563	          \((\mathrm{chart}_0, \mathrm{chart}_1) \colon
1564	            \mathrm{pullback}\,((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_0.f)\,
1565	              ((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_1.f)
1566	            \longrightarrow
1567	            \overline{\mathbb P^1} \times_{\bar k} \overline{\mathbb P^1}\)
1568	          via \(\mathtt{prod.lift}\) on the two named chart restrictions above.
1569	        \item Show this pair factors through \(\Delta_{\overline{\mathbb P^1}}\)
1570	          via the universal property of the intersection's pullback structure
1571	          combined with \(\mathtt{IsClosedImmersion.lift}\) (kernel-inequality
1572	          form; the \(\mathtt{\_iff\_range\_subset}\) variant is NOT in Mathlib
1573	          at \texttt{b80f227} per iter-188 verification, so this step requires

1577	          composition with \(\Delta\) equals \((\mathrm{chart}_0,
1578	          \mathrm{chart}_1)\).

1581	          \(\overline{\mathbb P^1} \times_{\bar k} \overline{\mathbb P^1}\), to
1582	          two scheme-equalities on the components --- each of which is a direct
1583	          chart-bridge equality on \((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_i\)'s structure

1591	      \(\mathtt{IsClosedImmersion.lift}\) (kernel-inequality form),

1614	  \noindent\textbf{(IV) Substantive ring-level residual.} All three pickup paths reduce,
1615	  after their structural setup, to the same ring-level identity. On the basic-open
1616	  intersection \(D_+(X_0 \cdot X_1) \subseteq \overline{\mathbb P^1}\) both chart

1618	  coordinate \(u = X_0/X_1\) are reciprocals: \(t \cdot u = 1\)), and the agreement

3	\chapter{The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) and its global sections (RR.3)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of \(\mathscr L(D)\)); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors
8	% as divisors of zeros of global sections); IV.1, pp.~294--297
9	% (Riemann--Roch, Lemma~1.2, Examples~1.3.3--1.3.5)
10	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161,
11	% 174, 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks
12	% below were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images
13	% of the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
14	
15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
23	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\), the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z\(, and the divisor \)[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, REF); the global-sections
35	calculation \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a smooth
36	proper genus-\(0\) curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) associated to a closed point \(P \in C\)
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its \(\bar k\)-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\)
50	in the genus-\(0\) specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that \(C\) admits a non-constant rational function \(f\)
52	having at most a simple pole at \(P\) and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	\(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) whose degree-\(1\) identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*\)
64	% to vanish \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) from the degree count
65	% \(\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0\). Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-\(0\) hypothesis
75	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0\) and the skyscraper
76	% identity \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\). The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-\(g\)
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
82	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor \([P]\) of a closed point is the one of
94	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order \(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	REF and REF.

158	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}

162	  \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf} satisfies
163	  \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(\bot) = K(C)\) (since the order conditions
164	  are vacuously satisfied on the empty open). This violates the sheaf
165	  axiom on the Grothendieck topology of opens: the empty sieve covers

169	  \[
170	    \mathrm{carrierSubmoduleSheaf}_P(U) \;:=\;

172	    \qquad \mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U \;:=\;

176	  \(F(U)\) on non-empty \(U\) (since then \(\mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U = \top\)).

196	  % sheaf \(\mathscr L(D)\) associated to a Cartier divisor) (read from
197	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
198	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
199	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
200	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
201	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
202	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
203	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
204	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
205	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
206	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.13. \emph{Let \(X\) be a scheme. Then:
207	  % (a) for any Cartier divisor \(D\), \(\mathscr L(D)\) is an invertible
208	  % sheaf on \(X\).}"
209	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of
210	  \(\mathscr L(D)\) and Proposition~6.13(a)).}
211	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
212	  closed point. Let \(\mathfrak m_P \subset \mathcal O_{C, P}\) denote the
213	  maximal ideal of the local ring at \(P\); by smoothness of \(C\),
214	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation ring and \(\mathfrak m_P\)
215	  is generated by any uniformizer \(f_P \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\) (an
216	  element of \(\mathfrak m_P \setminus \mathfrak m_P^2\)). The Cartier
217	  divisor associated to the closed point \(P\) is the effective
218	  Cartier divisor cut out locally by the uniformizer \(f_P\) on an open
219	  affine neighbourhood of \(P\), and by the unit \(1\) on the complement
220	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). The \emph{line bundle of the closed point \(P\)},
221	  denoted \(\mathcal O_C(P)\), is the locally free \(\mathcal O_C\)-module
222	  of rank \(1\) defined by Hartshorne's construction \(\mathscr L(D)\)
223	  applied to this Cartier divisor: explicitly, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
224	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings
225	  \(\mathcal K_C\) (the constant sheaf of the function field \(K(C)\) on the
226	  integral scheme \(C\)) that is generated by \(f_P^{-1} \in K(C)\) on a
227	  neighbourhood of \(P\) and by \(1 \in K(C)\) on the complement
228	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) confirms that
229	  this subsheaf is an invertible sheaf, independently of the choice of
230	  uniformizer \(f_P\) (any two uniformizers differ by a unit in
231	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}^{\times}\), so they generate the same
232	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule of \(\mathcal K_C\)).
233	
234	  \paragraph{Equivalent description via the ideal sheaf.} Let
235	  \(\mathcal I_P \subset \mathcal O_C\) denote the ideal sheaf of the
236	  closed point \(P\) (locally generated by the uniformizer \(f_P\) near
237	  \(P\), and equal to \(\mathcal O_C\) on the complement). Then
238	  \(\mathcal I_P\) is the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(-P)\) associated to the
239	  Weil divisor \(-[P]\), and \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
240	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-linear dual:
241	  \[
242	    \mathcal O_C(P) \;\cong\; \mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
243	    \mathcal O_C)
244	    \;\cong\; \mathcal I_P^{-1}.
245	  \]
246	  Both descriptions yield the same invertible sheaf; the
247	  ``subsheaf of \(\mathcal K_C\)'' description above is the one used in
248	  the prose, while the ``dual of the ideal sheaf'' description is the
249	  most direct entry point for the Lean construction (Mathlib's
250	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData} packages
251	  quasi-coherent ideal sheaves, from which the dual is constructed via
252	  the internal-hom on invertible sheaves).
253	
254	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
255	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint} takes a
256	  scheme \(C\), a point \(P : C\), and a closedness witness
257	  \(\texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}\) (the same input shape as
258	  REF), and returns an invertible
259	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module \(\mathcal O_C(P)\). The well-definedness as an
260	  invertible sheaf is part of the construction and goes through
261	  smoothness of \(C\) (delivering the DVR stalk at \(P\)) and the standard
262	  ideal-sheaf-of-a-closed-point machinery in Mathlib's
263	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf} chapter. The chapter's
264	  consumption of the closed-point hypothesis matches \texttt{RR.1}:
265	  closed plus dimension-\(1\)-integral delivers \(\mathrm{codim}_C \{P\} = 1\)
266	  automatically. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
267	\end{definition}

4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, IV \S1, pp.~294--297
7	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages
8	% 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below

12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter is the second of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
15	that close the project's headline RR bridge
16	\[
17	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
18	  \,\colon\,
19	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
20	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
21	\]
22	The bridge consumed by
23	REF of
24	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} is the Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain:
25	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) and the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
26	\to \mathbb Z$ (\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the
27	Riemann--Roch dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
28	\[
29	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
30	\]
31	(this chapter, \texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections identification

42	% \(H^i(X, \mathcal F) \cong H^{n-i}(X, \omega_X \otimes \mathcal F^\vee)^*\);

47	% standard preadditive-category fact), the genus base case
48	% \(\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g\), and the closed-point skyscraper
49	% identity \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\).

85	\section{The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve}
86	
87	\begin{definition}
88	
89	
90	\leanok
91	[Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve]
92	  \label{def:eulerChar_curve}
93	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic}
94	  
95	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(\chi(\mathcal F)\)
96	  % inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
97	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
98	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
99	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
100	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
101	  % is the Euler characteristic
102	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
103	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295, the formula displayed
104	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3.}
105	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F\)
106	  be a coherent sheaf on \(C\). The \emph{Euler characteristic} of
107	  \(\mathcal F\) is the integer
108	  \[
109	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;:=\;
110	    \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \,\dim_{\bar k} H^i(C, \mathcal F)
111	    \;=\;
112	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal F)
113	    \,-\,
114	    \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal F).
115	  \]
116	  The second equality is a curve-specific simplification: because
117	  \(\dim C = 1\), Grothendieck vanishing (Hartshorne~III.2.7) gives
118	  \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\), so the alternating sum has
119	  only two terms. Coherence of \(\mathcal F\) on the proper $\bar
120	  k\(-scheme \)C\( guarantees that both \)H^0\( and \)H^1$ are
121	  finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology
122	  finiteness theorem, the same finiteness backing
123	  REF), so the two terms are honest natural numbers and
124	  the difference is a well-defined integer.
125	
126	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
127	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic} takes a coherent
128	  sheaf \(\mathcal F\) on \(C\) and returns an integer; the natural input
129	  shape is the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
130	  wrapper \(\HModule \bar k\) (REF) applied to a
131	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured carrier built from \(\mathcal F\), with
132	  \(\dim_{\bar k}\) extracted via \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\). The
133	  declaration is \texttt{noncomputable} (inherited from
134	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) and from the \(\Ext\)-based cohomology
135	  construction).
136	\end{definition}

138	\section{The \(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor}
139	
140	\begin{definition}
141	
142	
143	\leanok
144	[\(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor]
145	  \label{def:l_invariant}
146	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l}
147	  
148	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\) and of the
149	  % "dimension of \(|D|\)") (read from
150	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
151	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We denote \(\dim_k H^0(X, \mathscr L(D))\) by \(l(D)\),
152	  % so that the \emph{dimension of \(|D|\)} is \(l(D) - 1\). The number
153	  % \(l(D)\) is finite by (II, 5.19) or (III, 5.2)."
154	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\)).}
155	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let
156	  \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor (REF). The
157	  \emph{\(\ell\)-invariant of \(D\)} is the integer
158	  \[
159	    \ell(D) \;:=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
160	    \;\in\; \mathbb N,
161	  \]
162	  the \(\bar k\)-dimension of the space of global sections of the
163	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to \(D\). Finiteness of
164	  \(\ell(D)\) is a consequence of the coherent-cohomology finiteness
165	  theorem on a proper \(\bar k\)-scheme (Hartshorne~II.5.19 or
166	  III.5.2). The classical \emph{complete linear system \(|D|\)} has
167	  \(\bar k\)-dimension \(\ell(D) - 1\) as a projective space, but the
168	  chapter never uses the projective-space interpretation; \(\ell(D)\) is
169	  the only quantity it needs.
170	
171	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
172	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l} accepts a divisor
173	  \(D : C.\mathtt{WeilDivisor}\) (REF) and returns
174	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\;(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)))\),
175	  computed through the same \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
176	  pipeline as REF. The associated invertible
177	  sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is the line bundle of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
178	  chapter, to be added); the present chapter's prose and Lean
179	  signature reference \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) by name and consume only its
180	  \(H^0\) functor. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
181	\end{definition}

183	\section{The \(\chi\) identity: \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\)}
184	
185	\subsection*{Substrate lemmas for the proof of the \(\chi\)-identity}
186	
187	The proof of REF below
188	decomposes into three substantive substrate lemmas, factored out of the
189	Hartshorne~IV.1.3 inductive argument. The first
190	(REF, \emph{additivity}) is the
191	classical statement that \(\chi\) descends to the Grothendieck group
192	of \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\); the second
193	(REF, \emph{iso-invariance}) is the
194	elementary fact that \(\chi\) is well defined on isomorphism classes
195	of sheaves; the third
196	(REF, \emph{skyscraper}) computes \(\chi\)
197	on a closed-point skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\). Pinning each as its own
198	Lean declaration lets the prover attack the three substrate gaps
199	independently of the parent inductive argument.
200	
201	\begin{lemma}\leanok
202	[Additivity of the Euler characteristic on a short exact sequence]
203	  \label{lem:euler_char_shortExact_add}
204	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add}
205	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
206	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (inside the proof of Theorem~1.3,
207	  % the citation ``III, Ex.~5.1'' for the additivity of \(\chi\) on a
208	  % short exact sequence) (read from
209	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
211	  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
212	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
213	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (citation of III, Ex.~5.1
214	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
215	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
216	  \(\bar k\), and let
217	  \[
218	    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
219	  \]
220	  be a short exact sequence in the category of \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
221	  sheaves on \(C\). Then
222	  \[
223	    \chi(\mathcal F_2) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3).
224	  \]
225	\end{lemma}
226	
227	\begin{proof}
228	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
229	  Apply the project-side \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured covariant Ext-LES
230	  (Mathlib's \texttt{Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence}) to the constant-sheaf
231	  test object \(L = (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
232	  \bar k\;\bar k)\) and the short exact sequence
233	  \(0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0\). The
234	  resulting nine-term sequence
235	  \[
236	    H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to H^0(\mathcal F_3)
237	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2) \to H^1(\mathcal F_3)
238	    \to H^2(\mathcal F_1) \to H^2(\mathcal F_2) \to H^2(\mathcal F_3)
239	  \]
240	  is exact in the category of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces. Grothendieck
241	  vanishing \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\) on the
242	  one-dimensional scheme \(C\) (Hartshorne~III.2.7) truncates the
243	  sequence to a six-term exact sequence
244	  \[
245	    0 \to K_0 \to H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to
246	    H^0(\mathcal F_3) \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2)
247	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_3) \to 0
248	  \]
249	  (with \(K_0 = 0\) the kernel of the first map, kept here only as a
250	  bookkeeping placeholder). Rank-counting on a six-term exact sequence
251	  of finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces — iterated applications
252	  of \(\mathtt{Submodule.finrank\_quotient\_add\_finrank}\) at each
253	  intermediate kernel/image quotient — yields the alternating identity
254	  \[
255	    \dim H^0(\mathcal F_1) - \dim H^0(\mathcal F_2) + \dim H^0(\mathcal F_3)
256	    - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_1) + \dim H^1(\mathcal F_2) - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_3)
257	    \;=\; 0,
258	  \]
259	  which rearranges, using the definition
260	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
261	  (REF), to
262	  \(\chi(\mathcal F_2) = \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3)\).
263	
264	  % NOTE: gated on project-side LES carrier + finiteness machinery.
265	  % Mathlib `b80f227` ships `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` but not (a)
266	  % the `ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured LES carrier specialised to the
267	  % project's sheaf-cohomology pipeline `Scheme.HModule`, (b) Grothendieck
268	  % vanishing `H^i(C, F) = 0` for `i ≥ 2` at the `Ext`-cohomology level
269	  % consumed by `HModule`, nor (c) the six-term rank-counting bridge
270	  % composed of `Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank` invocations.
271	  % The Lean helper `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` therefore
272	  % carries a Tier-3 honest typed sorry; its closure lands once the
273	  % three substrate pieces are in place. Off the genus-0 critical path
274	  % (the parent theorem `thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus`
275	  % is the only consumer in `RR.2`).
276	\end{proof}

3	\chapter{The invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) of a Weil divisor (RR.2\(_\ast\))}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OcOfD}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II~\S6, pp.~144--145
7	% (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\); Proposition 6.13; Remark 6.17.1;
8	% Proposition 6.18) and IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive-step short exact
9	% sequence). Read directly from
10	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162 and
11	% 313, by rendering the scanned page images and transcribing the
12	% statements character-by-character (the PDF has no text layer). Stacks
13	% pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (Weil divisors), 0AUW
14	% (the sheaf \(\mathcal O_X(D)\) from a Weil divisor), 0BE3 (degree-zero of
15	% a principal divisor on a complete nonsingular curve).
16	
17	\section{Setup and motivation}
18	
19	This chapter is a satellite of \texttt{RR.2}
20	(REF); together with \texttt{RR.1}
21	(REF), \texttt{RR.3}
22	(REF), and \texttt{RR.4}
23	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex}) it forms the four-stage
24	sub-build closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
25	\[
26	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
27	  \,\colon\,
28	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
29	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
30	\]
31	The Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain consumed by REF
32	of \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} relies on the invertible-sheaf
33	functor
34	\[
35	  \mathcal O_C(-) \;\colon\; \mathrm{Div}(C) \;\longrightarrow\;
36	  \mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})
37	\]
38	sending a Weil divisor \(D = \sum_i n_i [P_i]\) to its associated invertible
39	sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) on \(C\). The Lean entry-point
40	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} of
41	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} (line~168) currently
42	encodes this functor as a typed-\texttt{sorry} \texttt{noncomputable def}
43	with no chapter pinning. This chapter supplies the missing pinning by
44	blueprinting the Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}
45	(to be created in iter-183), where the body of \texttt{sheafOf} and its
46	three immediate corollaries will live.
47	
48	\paragraph{Why this chapter is gated to \texttt{RR.2}.} The
49	\(\chi\)-identity proof of REF
50	in \texttt{RR.2} is an induction on the Weil divisor \(D\) via the
51	``\(D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]\)'' step of Hartshorne~IV.1.3. That step
52	intrinsically references the body of \texttt{sheafOf}: it asks for the
53	short exact sequence
54	\[
55	  0 \to \mathcal O_C(D) \to \mathcal O_C(D + [Y]) \to k(Y)^{[\text{at } Y]}
56	  \to 0
57	\]
58	to be exact in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\), which is not a statement on the
59	\emph{signature} of \texttt{sheafOf} but on its \emph{body}. The iter-181
60	Lane H factoring of \texttt{RR.2} consequently
61	landed two helper sorries
62	(\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_zero},
63	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_succ}) that are
64	\emph{intrinsically gated} on the body of \texttt{sheafOf}. Until this
65	chapter pins the body, those helpers remain vacuous regardless of how
66	much \(\chi\)-machinery \texttt{RR.2} adds on top.
67	
68	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.3}.} The
69	existing \texttt{RR.3} chapter (REF) constructs
70	the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) associated to a \emph{closed point}
71	\(P \in C\) directly, bypassing the Weil-divisor packaging. The
72	REF below makes this bypass mathematically
73	coherent by exhibiting \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) at \(D = [P]\) as canonically
74	isomorphic to REF. With this isomorphism in
75	hand, \texttt{RR.3}'s downstream consumption of
76	\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via the
77	\(\chi\)-identity routes through \texttt{sheafOf} cleanly.
78	
79	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.4}.} The
80	two pins consumed by the \texttt{RR.4} chapter
81	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex})
82	that translate Riemann--Roch outputs into the function-field/degree
83	language of \(C\) both reference \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) for general \(D\) (not
84	just \(D = [P]\)): the function-field-degree bridge takes a divisor
85	\(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) and reads off \(\deg(D)\) from the
86	\texttt{sheafOf}-associated invertible sheaf, while the
87	Weil-divisor / line-bundle correspondence is the substance of
88	Hartshorne's Proposition~6.15 (which the present chapter pins via
89	REF).
90	
91	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
92	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
93	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
94	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}) carry the same
95	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
96	\begin{quote}
97	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
98	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
99	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
100	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
101	\end{quote}
102	The Weil divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) is the one defined in
103	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex}; the
104	prime-divisor data type is REF; the order
105	\(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor \(Q\) is REF; the
106	principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a non-zero rational function is
107	REF; the closed-point divisor \([P]\) is
108	REF. The line bundle at a closed point
109	already constructed in \texttt{RR.3} is
110	REF, and the present chapter's
111	REF makes the comparison
112	\(\mathcal O_C([P]) = \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}\) explicit.
113	
114	\paragraph{Route alignment with iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom}
115	analogy.} The iter-182 api-alignment analogy
116	\texttt{analogies/ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md} surveyed three candidate
117	Mathlib paths for the closed-point specialisation \(\mathcal O_C([P])\):
118	(i)~\texttt{Sheaf-internal-Hom} on \(\mathbf{SheafOfModules}\) (rejected:
119	no \texttt{IdealSheafData \(\to\) SheafOfModules} realiser in Mathlib,
120	no \(\mathbf{SheafOfModules}\) dual functor in Mathlib);
121	(ii)~the abstract dual $\mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
122	\mathcal O_C)$ in a new \texttt{IdealSheafDual.lean} (rejected: high
123	LOC cost in exchange for a single sheaf the project can build directly);
124	(iii)~Hartshorne's \emph{subsheaf-of-\(\mathcal K_C\)} description (accepted).
125	The present chapter adopts route~(iii) for the general
126	\texttt{sheafOf \(D\)} as well, and REF verifies
127	that the general construction at \(D = [P]\) recovers the closed-point
128	specialisation of \texttt{RR.3} on the nose. The subsheaf-of-\(\mathcal K_C\)
129	recipe is mathematically Hartshorne's original (II~\S6, the definition of
130	\(\mathscr L(D)\)) and was equally available for the closed-point case; the
131	project's choice to use it both at the general \(D\) and at the \(D = [P]\)
132	specialisation is what makes the comparison
133	\(\mathcal O_C([P]) \cong \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}\) a direct unfolding
134	rather than a non-trivial isomorphism.

136	\section{The invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)}
137	
138	\begin{definition}\leanok
139	[Invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) of a Weil divisor]
140	  \label{def:sheafOf}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf}
142	  
143	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
144	  % sheaf \(\mathscr L(D)\) associated to a Cartier divisor on a scheme),
145	  % and Remark~6.17.1, Proposition~6.18 + Proposition~6.15, p.~145
146	  % (Cartier \(\leftrightarrow\) Weil correspondence on an integral
147	  % locally factorial scheme; identification of \(\mathscr L(D)\) as a
148	  % subsheaf of the constant sheaf at the function field \(K\)). Read from
149	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162,
150	  % transcribed character-by-character from rendered scanned page
151	  % images (the PDF has no text layer).
152	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
153	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
154	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
155	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
156	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
157	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), so \(f_i^{-1}\) and
158	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
159	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
160	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.15. \emph{If \(X\) is an integral
161	  % scheme, the homomorphism \(\mathrm{CaCl}\, X \to \mathrm{Pic}\, X\) of
162	  % (6.14) is an isomorphism.} Proof. We have only to show that every
163	  % invertible sheaf is isomorphic to a subsheaf of \(\mathscr K\), which
164	  % in this case is the constant sheaf \(K\), where \(K\) is the function
165	  % field of \(X\)."
166	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Remark 6.17.1. Clearly this gives a 1-1
167	  % correspondence between effective Cartier divisors on \(X\) and
168	  % \emph{locally principal} closed subschemes \(Y\), i.e., subschemes
169	  % whose sheaf of ideals is locally generated by a single element.
170	  % Note also that if \(X\) is an integral separated noetherian locally
171	  % factorial scheme, so that the Cartier divisors correspond to Weil
172	  % divisors by (6.11), then the effective Cartier divisors correspond
173	  % exactly to the effective Weil divisors."
174	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (the definition of
175	  \(\mathscr L(D)\)), Proposition~6.15 (the constant-sheaf-at-\(K\)
176	  identification on an integral scheme), and Remark~6.17.1 (Weil
177	  divisors and Cartier divisors agree on an integral separated
178	  noetherian locally factorial scheme).}
179	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
180	  \(\bar k\) and let \(D = \sum_{Q} n_Q [Q] \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor
181	  on \(C\), with the formal sum indexed by prime divisors \(Q\) of \(C\) and
182	  supported on a finite set \(\{Q : n_Q \neq 0\}\)
183	  (REF). The \emph{invertible sheaf
184	  \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to \(D\)}, also written
185	  \(\mathcal O_C(D) := \mathscr L(D)\) in Hartshorne's notation, is the
186	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the constant sheaf
187	  \(\mathcal K_C \cong K(C)\) (Proposition~6.15: on an integral scheme the
188	  sheaf of total quotient rings is the constant sheaf at the function
189	  field) defined section-wise by
190	  \[
191	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(D)\bigr) \;:=\;
192	    \{\, 0 \,\} \;\cup\;
193	    \bigl\{\, f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\;
194	      \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; -\,n_Q \text{ for every prime divisor }
195	      Q \text{ of } C \text{ with } Q \in U \bigr\}
196	  \]
197	  for each open \(U \subseteq C\). Restriction along \(V \hookrightarrow U\)
198	  is the identity on the underlying carrier \(K(C)\), which preserves
199	  membership (a smaller open imposes \emph{fewer} order constraints, so
200	  the constraint set on \(U\) embeds into the constraint set on \(V\)). The
201	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on
202	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) is restricted from the
203	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on \(K(C)\) (a \(\bar k\)-scalar multiple of a
204	  rational function has the same order at every prime divisor).
205	
206	  \paragraph{Equivalence with the Cartier-divisor description.} The
207	  formal sum \(D = \sum_Q n_Q [Q]\) packages, on a curve where prime
208	  divisors are exactly closed points and the local rings at prime
209	  divisors are DVRs (smoothness \(+\) dimension~\(1\)), the Cartier-divisor
210	  data \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) of Hartshorne's definition: locally on a small
211	  open \(U_i\) containing exactly one prime divisor \(Q\) in the support of
212	  \(D\), pick a uniformiser \(\pi_Q \in K(C)\) at \(Q\) and set
213	  \(f_i := \pi_Q^{n_Q}\); on opens disjoint from the support of \(D\), set
214	  \(f_i := 1\). The transitions on overlaps \(U_i \cap U_j\) are units in
215	  \(\mathcal O_{C, Q}^{\times}\) for each prime \(Q\) in the overlap (any
216	  two uniformisers differ by a unit, by the DVR structure), so the
217	  \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) define a Cartier divisor whose Weil-divisor image
218	  under Hartshorne's (6.11) is the original \(D\). The
219	  ``\(f_i^{-1}\)-generated'' subsheaf of \(\mathcal K_C\) from Hartshorne's
220	  definition then coincides with the section-wise description above:
221	  the local generator \(f_i^{-1} = \pi_Q^{-n_Q}\) has order \(-n_Q\) at \(Q\),
222	  so the local sections are exactly \(f \in K(C)\) with
223	  \(\ord_Q(f \cdot \pi_Q^{n_Q}) \geq 0\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q\), on
224	  \(U_i\).
225	
226	  Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) (quoted in
227	  REF of REF)
228	  then guarantees that the resulting subsheaf $\mathcal O_C(D) \subset
229	  \mathcal K_C\( is an invertible sheaf on \)C$, independently of the
230	  choice of uniformisers \(\pi_Q\) (any two choices differ by a unit, so
231	  they generate the same \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule).
232	
233	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
234	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} takes a Weil
235	  divisor \(D : C.\text{left.WeilDivisor}\) and returns a
236	  \(\texttt{Sheaf}~J~(\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\) on
237	  \(C.\text{left.toTopCat}\), where
238	  \(J = \texttt{Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat}\) is the
239	  standard topology on the topological space underlying \(C\). The
240	  closed-point specialisation \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) is
241	  REF of
242	  REF; their agreement at \(D = [P]\) is
243	  REF below. The construction follows
244	  the iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom} recipe in
245	  \texttt{analogies/}: presheaf-by-hand via per-open

258	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
259	  \(\bar k\). Then the invertible sheaf associated to the zero divisor
260	  \(0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) is canonically the structure sheaf:
261	  \[

271	  section condition of REF reduces, on every open
272	  \(U \subseteq C\), to
273	  \[
274	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(0)\bigr)
275	    \;=\; \{ 0 \} \cup \{ f \in K(C)^{\times} \;|\;
276	      \ord_Q(f) \geq 0 \text{ for every prime divisor } Q \in U \}.
277	  \]
278	  By the local description of the structure sheaf on the integral
279	  scheme \(C\) (the regular functions on \(U\) are exactly the rational
280	  functions whose order is non-negative at every prime divisor of
281	  \(C\) landing in \(U\); this is the standard ``non-negative order \(=\)
282	  regular'' identification, e.g.\ Hartshorne~II~\S6 immediately before
283	  Proposition~6.11), the right-hand side is precisely
284	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)\). The restriction maps on both sides are
285	  the identity on \(K(C)\), so the equality of presheaves promotes to an
286	  equality of sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\).
287	\end{proof}

1883	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1884	  \textbf{Off the genus-\(0\) critical path (iter-164).} This \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0\)
1885	  lemma is \emph{superseded by the \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut}
1886	  (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}), which closes the
1887	  base case without it. It is retained only as the classical Milne Proposition~3.9 ($\mathbb
1888	  G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility) argument feeding the alternative route
1889	  \cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}; no block on the genus-\(0\) critical path depends on it.
1890	  Let \(A\) be an abelian variety over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let
1891	  \(\varphi \colon \mathbb G_a \to A\) be a homomorphism of group objects (so \(\varphi(0) = 0_A\) and
1892	  \(\varphi(x + y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y)\)). Then \(\varphi\) is the trivial homomorphism: it
1893	  factors through the terminal object as the constant morphism at the identity,
1894	  \(\varphi = \mathtt{toUnit}\,\mathbb G_a \fatsemi \eta_A\).
1895	  \medskip
1896	  \noindent\textbf{Equivalent completeness phrasing.} Equivalently: a homomorphism from an
1897	  \emph{affine connected} group object into a \emph{complete} group object is trivial. That is the
1898	  abstract content; the proof below realises it characteristic-free.
1899	\end{lemma}
1900	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. According to (3.2), α extends to a regular map on the whole of A¹.
1901	% After composing α with a translation, we may suppose that α(0)=0. Then α is a homomorphism,
1902	% α(x+y)=α(x)+α(y); all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. But A¹−{0} is also a group variety, and similarly,
1903	% α(xy)=α(x)+α(y)+c; all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. This is absurd, unless α is constant." [Milne,
1904	% Prop 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20.]
1905	\begin{proof}
1906	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1907	  Write \(A\) additively (an abelian variety is commutative). We give the conceptual
1908	  \emph{completeness} proof as the abstract argument, then record Milne's elementary $\mathbb
1909	  G_a/\mathbb G_m\( incompatibility, which applies whenever \)\varphi$ extends to a morphism on
1910	  \(\mathbb P^1\) (as it does in the genus-\(0\) application).
1911	  \textbf{Completeness proof.} The image \(\varphi(\mathbb G_a)\) is a connected subgroup of \(A\)
1912	  (image of the connected \(\mathbb G_a\) under a homomorphism). As the image of a homomorphism of
1913	  algebraic groups it is a \emph{closed} subgroup of \(A\), hence \emph{complete} (closed in the
1914	  complete \(A\)); as a homomorphic image of the \emph{affine} \(\mathbb G_a\) it is moreover
1915	  \emph{affine}. A connected variety that is simultaneously complete and affine over \(\bar k\) is a
1916	  single point: its ring of global regular functions is a field finite over \(\bar k\), hence equal
1917	  to \(\bar k\) (the \(\Gamma = \bar k\) collapse already packaged for proper integral sources in
1918	  \cref{lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}). Therefore \(\varphi(\mathbb G_a)\) is one point;
1919	  being a homomorphism, \(\varphi\) sends \(0\) there, so the point is \(0_A\) and
1920	  \(\varphi = \mathtt{toUnit}\,\mathbb G_a \fatsemi \eta_A\).
1921	  \medskip
1922	  \noindent\textbf{Milne's elementary \(\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m\) incompatibility (when \(\varphi\)
1923	  extends to \(\mathbb P^1\)).} Suppose \(\varphi = f|_{\mathbb G_a}\) for a morphism
1924	  \(f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\) (the genus-\(0\) situation). Set \(g := f - f(1)\), so \(g(1) = 0_A\), and
1925	  form \(h_\times := \sigma_\times \fatsemi g \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A\) with the
1926	  scaling companion \(\sigma_\times\) of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}. Applying the additive
1927	  decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} with \(V = \mathbb P^1\) (complete),
1928	  \(W = \mathbb G_m\), base points \(1 \in \mathbb P^1\) and \(1 \in \mathbb G_m\) (so
1929	  \(h_\times(1, 1) = g(1) = 0_A\)), the axis restrictions are \(x \mapsto g(x)\) and
1930	  \(\lambda \mapsto g(\lambda)\), whence \(g(\lambda x) = g(x) + g(\lambda)\) on \(\bar k^\times\);
1931	  re-expanding \(g\) gives the multiplicative relation
1932	  \(\varphi(xy) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y) + c\) with \(c = -\varphi(1)\). Now \(\varphi\) is at once
1933	  additive, \(\varphi(x + y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y)\), and ``multiplicative-additive'',
1934	  \(\varphi(xy) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y) + c\). Computing \(\varphi((x + x')\,y)\) in two ways for
1935	  \(x, x' \in \bar k\), \(y \in \bar k^\times\),
1936	  \[ \varphi((x+x')y) = \varphi(xy) + \varphi(x'y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(x') + 2\varphi(y) + 2c, \]
1937	  while also $\varphi((x+x')y) = \varphi(x+x') + \varphi(y) + c = \varphi(x) + \varphi(x') +
1938	  \varphi(y) + c\(; equating gives \)\varphi(y) = -c = \varphi(1)\( for every \)y \in \bar k^\times$.
1939	  Hence \(\varphi\) is constant on the dense \(\mathbb G_m \subset \mathbb G_a\), so constant on
1940	  \(\mathbb G_a\) (density, \(A\) separated), with value \(\varphi(0) = 0_A\). This is Milne's argument
1941	  verbatim and uses only the proven \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} plus the scaling action,
1942	  avoiding the ``image is a closed subgroup'' input of the completeness proof. \qedhere
1943	\end{proof}

2001	  \textbf{Off the genus-\(0\) critical path (iter-164): retained alternative route.} This

2003	  shortcut} now installed as the proof of \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} (see
2004	  \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). It is retained as the classical Milne Proposition~3.9
2005	  argument (faithful to the \(\mathbb G_a \to A\) homomorphism / \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0\)
2006	  presentation), but the live consumer \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} no longer depends on
2007	  it; it is provided here only for completeness.
2008	  Let \(A\) be an abelian variety over \(\bar k\) and \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) a
2009	  morphism with \(f(0) = 0_A\) (the additive-identity \(\bar k\)-point of $\mathbb G_a = \mathbb A^1
2010	  \subset \mathbb P^1\(; any base point reduces to this by a normalising translation in \)A$).
2011	  Then \(f\) is constant. Concretely: the translation action \(\sigma\) (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1})
2012	  makes \(f|_{\mathbb G_a}\) an additive-group homomorphism \(\mathbb G_a \to A\), and a homomorphism
2013	  \(\mathbb G_a \to A\) is trivial (\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}), so \(f\) collapses to the
2014	  constant \(0_A\).
2015	\end{lemma}
2016	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. According to (3.2), α extends to a regular map on the whole of A¹.
2017	% After composing α with a translation, we may suppose that α(0)=0. Then α is a homomorphism,
2018	% α(x+y)=α(x)+α(y); all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. But A¹−{0} is also a group variety, and similarly,
2019	% α(xy)=α(x)+α(y)+c; all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. This is absurd, unless α is constant." [Milne,
2020	% Prop 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20.]
2021	\begin{proof}
2022	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
2023	  Normalise \(f(0) = 0_A\) by a translation in \(A\) (un-translate at the end). The proof is the
2024	  \emph{4th route} (\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}): the additive defect map is never built on
2025	  the affine surface and never extended; instead we precompose \(f\) with the \emph{total}
2026	  translation action \(\sigma\), and the complete factor is \(\mathbb P^1\) itself, so the proven
2027	  additive decomposition applies directly. Milne's Theorem~3.2
2028	  (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is \emph{not} used.
2029	  \textbf{\(f|_{\mathbb G_a}\) is an additive homomorphism, via the total action \(\sigma\).} Form
2030	  \[ h := \sigma \fatsemi f \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to A, \qquad
2031	     \text{``}h(x, y) = f(x + y)\text{''}, \]
2032	  using the translation action \(\sigma\) of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}; this is a \emph{total}
2033	  morphism (no rational-map extension needed). Apply the additive decomposition
2034	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (Milne Cor~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires only the
2035	  \emph{first} factor complete --- with \(V = \mathbb P^1\) (proper), \(W = \mathbb G_a\), and base
2036	  points \(0 \in \mathbb P^1\), \(0 \in \mathbb G_a\), noting \(h(0, 0) = f(0) = 0_A\). The axis
2037	  restrictions are \(x \mapsto h(x, 0) = f(x)\) and \(y \mapsto h(0, y) = f(y)\), so the decomposition
2038	  \(h = (p \fatsemi f) + (q \fatsemi (f|_{\mathbb G_a}))\) reads, on \(\bar k\)-points,
2039	  \[ f(x + y) = f(x) + f(y), \qquad x \in \mathbb P^1(\bar k),\ y \in \mathbb G_a(\bar k) = \bar k. \]
2040	  Restricting \(x\) to \(\mathbb G_a\) shows \(f|_{\mathbb G_a} \colon \mathbb G_a \to A\) is a
2041	  homomorphism of group objects (it sends \(0 \mapsto 0_A\) and respects addition).
2042	  \textbf{A homomorphism \(\mathbb G_a \to A\) is trivial.} By \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}
2043	  (the \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0\) lemma, the content of Milne's \(\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m\)
2044	  incompatibility), \(f|_{\mathbb G_a}\) is the constant morphism at \(0_A\). Hence \(f\) is constant on
2045	  the dense open \(\mathbb G_a \subset \mathbb P^1\); since \(\mathbb G_a\) is dense and \(A\) is
2046	  separated, \(f\) is constant on all of \(\mathbb P^1\), with value \(f(0) = 0_A\) (i.e.\ the original
2047	  base value before the normalising translation). \qedhere
2048	  \medskip
2049	  \noindent\emph{Note: this route is the demoted alternative.} The primary base-case proof is the
2050	  \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut now installed directly as the proof of
2051	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} (\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}): the multiplicative
2052	  scaling action \(\sigma_\times\) of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1} gives $\sigma_\times \fatsemi f =
2053	  \mathrm{pr}_1 \fatsemi f\( (the \)W\(-axis collapses because \)0$ is a scaling fixed point), so
2054	  
2055	  \(f|_{\mathbb G_m}\) is constant and \(f\) is constant by density --- bypassing both the $\mathbb
2056	  G_a$-additivity step and \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, and avoiding the ``image is a closed
2057	  subgroup'' Mathlib gap. The present \(\mathbb G_a\)-additive lemma is retained only for fidelity to
2058	  \uses{lem:hom_additivity_over_product, def:gaTranslationP1, lem:gmScaling_fixes_zero}
2059	  Milne; it is not on the genus-\(0\) critical path.
2060	\end{proof}
2061	\section{Morphisms from \(\mathbb P^1\) to an abelian variety are constant}
2062	\begin{proposition}
2063	\leanok
2064	[A morphism \(\mathbb P^1 \to A\) is constant]
2065	  \label{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}
2066	  
2067	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const}
2068	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.10 (specialised to a single \(\mathbb P^1\) factor), \S I.3, doc p.~20 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 26)
2069	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.10. Every rational map \(\alpha \colon V \dashrightarrow A\)
2070	  % from a unirational variety to an abelian variety is constant."
2071	  % SOURCE QUOTE (unirational, same page): "A variety \(V\) over an algebraically closed field
2072	  % is said to be unirational if there is a dominating rational map $\mathbb A^n
2073	  % \dashrightarrow V\( with \)n = \dim V\(; equivalently, if \)k(V)$ can be embedded into
2074	  % \(k(X_1, \ldots, X_n)\) (pure transcendental extension of \(k\)). A variety \(V\) over an
2075	  % arbitrary field \(k\) is said to be unirational if \(V_{k^{al}}\) is unirational."
2076	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.10 (case \(V = \mathbb P^1\)).}
2077	  Let \(\bar k\) be algebraically closed and \(A\) an abelian variety over \(\bar k\). Then every
2078	  morphism \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) is constant: \(f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})\) is a
2079	  single \(\bar k\)-point.
2080	\end{proposition}
2081	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. We may suppose that \(k\) is algebraically closed. By assumption
2082	% there is a rational map \(\mathbb A^n \dashrightarrow V\) with dense image, and the composite
2083	% of this with \(\alpha\) extends to a morphism $\beta \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \cdots \times
2084	% \mathbb P^1 \to A$. An induction argument, starting from Corollary 1.5, shows that there are
2085	% regular maps \(\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\) such that $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum
2086	% \beta_i(x_i)\(, and the lemma shows that each \)\beta_i$ is constant."
2087	\begin{proof}
2088	  This is the single-factor (\(d = 1\)) instance of Milne's Proposition~3.10, where the
2089	  unirational variety is \(\mathbb P^1\) itself. We give the project's \textbf{primary} proof, the
2090	  \emph{\(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut}: it proves constancy \emph{in one shot} from the proven
2091	  additive decomposition (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) and a scaling fixed point,
2092	  without the intermediate ``\(f|_{\mathbb G_a}\) additive'' step and \emph{without} the
2093	  \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0\) triviality lemma. The theorem of the cube is \emph{not} used
2094	  (\cref{rmk:cube_not_needed}), and the map \(f\) is already a morphism out of the complete
2095	  nonsingular \(\mathbb P^1\), so Milne's Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is
2096	  \emph{not} invoked either.
2097	  \textbf{Reduction to a base point.} Choose the scaling fixed point $0 \in \mathbb G_a \subset
2098	  \mathbb P^1\( (one of the two fixed points of \)\sigma_\times\(). Translating in the group \)A$ by
2099	  \(-f(0)\), we may assume \(f(0) = 0_A\); un-translating at the end restores the original value.
2100	  \textbf{The scaling shortcut.} Form the \emph{total} morphism
2101	  \[ h := \sigma_\times \fatsemi f \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A, \qquad
2102	     \text{``}h(x, \lambda) = f(\lambda x)\text{''}, \]
2103	  using the multiplicative scaling action \(\sigma_\times\) of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1} (a genuine
2104	  total morphism --- no rational-map extension needed). Apply the additive decomposition
2105	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (Milne Cor~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires only the
2106	  \emph{first} factor complete --- with \(V = \mathbb P^1\) (proper), \(W = \mathbb G_m\), and base
2107	  points \(0 \in \mathbb P^1\), \(1 \in \mathbb G_m\); here \(h(0, 1) = f(1 \cdot 0) = f(0) = 0_A\), as
2108	  required. The decomposition writes \(h = (p \fatsemi f_0) + (q \fatsemi g)\) with \(f_0\) the
2109	  \(V\)-axis restriction \(x \mapsto h(x, 1) = f(1 \cdot x) = f(x)\) (so \(f_0 = f\)) and \(g\) the
2110	  \(W\)-axis restriction \(\lambda \mapsto h(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0) = 0_A\).
2111	  \textbf{The \(W\)-axis collapses (the load-bearing scaling fixed point).} Because \(0\) is a
2112	  \emph{fixed point} of scaling, \(\sigma_\times(0, \lambda) = 0\) for all \(\lambda\)
2113	  (\cref{lem:gmScaling_fixes_zero}), so the \(W\)-axis
2114	  restriction \(g\) is the \emph{constant} morphism at \(0_A\) --- the identity of the hom-group
2115	  \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_m, A)\). The corresponding summand \(q \fatsemi g\) is therefore the group
2116	  identity, and the decomposition collapses to
2117	  \[ h = p \fatsemi f, \qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad
2118	     \sigma_\times \fatsemi f = \mathrm{pr}_1 \fatsemi f, \qquad
2119	     \text{``}f(\lambda x) = f(x)\text{''} \quad (x \in \mathbb P^1,\ \lambda \in \mathbb G_m). \]
2120	  \textbf{Constancy via density.} Specialise \(x = 1\) (precompose with $\lambda \mapsto (1,
2121	  \lambda)\(): \)f(\lambda) = f(1)\( for all \)\lambda \in \mathbb G_m\(, i.e.\ \)f|_{\mathbb G_m}$ is
2122	  the constant morphism at \(f(1)\). Since \(\mathbb G_m\) is dense in the irreducible \(\mathbb P^1\)
2123	  and \(A\) is separated, the two morphisms \(f\) and \(\mathrm{const}_{f(1)}\), agreeing on the
2124	  non-empty open \(\mathbb G_m\), agree everywhere by the dominant-source/separated-target rigidity
2125	  handle \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} (the same \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant} family
2126	  \texttt{rigidity\_core} uses). Hence \(f\) is constant, with value \(f(1)\); in the normalised
2127	  picture \(f(0) = 0_A\), so the constant is \(0_A\) (the original \(f(0)\) before translation).
2128	  \medskip
2129	  \noindent\emph{Multi-factor remark.} For the general unirational statement of Proposition~3.10,
2130	  one writes \(\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum_i \beta_i(x_i)\) via the additive decomposition
2131	  (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) and applies this base case to each $\beta_i \colon
2132	  \mathbb P^1 \to A\(; we need only the \)d = 1\( case here. The Milne-faithful \)\mathbb
2133	  G_a\(-additive route (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}, via \)\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$,
2134	  \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}) reaches the same conclusion and is retained as an alternative,
2135	  but is \emph{not} on this critical path.

2190	\section{The headline: rigidity for a pointed genus-\(0\) curve}
2191	\begin{theorem}
2192	\leanok
2193	[Rigidity: a pointed morphism from a genus-\(0\) curve to an abelian variety is constant]
2194	  \label{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
2195	  
2196	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme}
2197	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field (\emph{arbitrary characteristic}; no
2198	  \(\mathtt{CharZero}\) hypothesis). Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
2199	  over \(\bar k\) with \(\genus(C) = 0\), and let \(A\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
2200	  group scheme over \(\bar k\) (an abelian variety) with identity \(\eta_A\). Then for every
2201	  morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) such that \(p \fatsemi f = \eta_A\) for some \(\bar k\)-point
2202	  \(p \colon \mathbf 1 \to C\), the morphism \(f\) equals the constant morphism at the identity,
2203	  \(f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A\).
2204	  This is the project's committed characteristic-free statement that the genus-\(0\)
2205	  Albanese-witness \(\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}\) consumes. Its signature mirrors
2206	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar}\) of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}
2207	  \emph{verbatim except} that the \([\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]\) instance is dropped: same
2208	  curve typeclasses (\(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1\), \(\mathtt{IsProper}\),
2209	  \(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\) on \(C\)), same abelian-variety typeclasses
2210	  (\(\mathtt{GrpObj}\), \(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{Smooth}\), \(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\)
2211	  on \(A\)), and the same pointed-morphism conclusion shape $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi
2212	  \eta[A]$.
2213	\end{theorem}
2214	\begin{proof}
2215	  By \cref{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1} the genus-\(0\) curve \(C\) over the algebraically closed
2216	  field \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) via some isomorphism
2217	  \(\varphi \colon C \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\). The composite
2218	  \(f \circ \varphi^{-1} \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) is then a morphism from
2219	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) to the abelian variety \(A\), hence constant by
2220	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}: its image is a single \(\bar k\)-point \(a_0 \in A\).
2221	  Therefore \(f\) itself is constant with value \(a_0\). The pointed hypothesis
2222	  \(p \fatsemi f = \eta_A\) evaluates this constant at the \(\bar k\)-point \(p\) and pins it to the
2223	  identity, \(a_0 = \eta_A\). Consequently \(f\) factors through the terminal object as the
2224	  constant morphism at the identity, \(f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A\).
2225	  No differential (\(df = 0\)) input, no \(H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0\) / Serre-duality decomposition,
2226	  and no Picard representability is used; the argument is valid in arbitrary characteristic.
2227	  Once formalised it lets the \([\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]\) hypothesis carried by
2228	  \(\mathtt{rigidity\_over\_kbar}\) be dropped, and (after the \(\bar k \to k\) descent step hosted
2229	  in \cref{chap:Jacobian}, not here) feeds \(\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}.\mathtt{key}\).
2230	  % NOTE (iter-166 review): proof body landed iter-166 via clean iso-transport
2231	  % (`obtain ⟨φ⟩ := genusZero_curve_iso_P1 _hgenus; set g := φ.inv ≫ f; obtain ⟨a₀, hga₀⟩ :=
2232	  % morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const g; pin a₀ = η[A] via `toUnit_unique` basepoint transport;
2233	  % back-transport via `φ.hom_inv_id`'). `lean_verify` axioms $\{\mathtt{propext},
2234	  % \mathtt{sorryAx}, \mathtt{Classical.choice}, \mathtt{Quot.sound}\}\( — \)\mathtt{sorryAx}$
2235	  % propagates honestly through (a) `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (RR-bridge sub-build, deferred to
2236	  % iter-167+) and (b) `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const`'s 5 helper sorries. Lifts to axiom-clean
2237	  % once both upstreams discharge; checker confirmed no laundering.
2238	\end{proof}

59	\section{Setup and statement}
60	\label{sec:albaneseup_setup}
61	
62	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
63	irreducible curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C) > 0\) (the positive-genus
64	hypothesis is essential: it is precisely the standing hypothesis of Milne's
65	\S III.6). Fix moreover a \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) to serve
66	as the basepoint of the Albanese construction.
67	
68	By the A.3 row of Route~A (the identity-component construction applied to the
69	representable Picard scheme of
70	\cref{def:pic_scheme} in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) the
71	identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \subseteq \Pic_{C/k}\) is a smooth proper
72	geometrically irreducible \(\bar k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\); we write
73	\(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) and let \(\eta_J \colon \Spec \bar k \to J\) denote its
74	identity section, in the project's convention the chosen \(\mathbf 1\)-pointed
75	morphism that represents the unit element of the underlying group object on
76	\(J\).
77	
78	The universal \(T\)-points of \(J\) are degree-\(0\) line bundles on $C \times_{\bar k}
79	T\(, taken modulo line bundles pulled back from \)T$ (\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback};
80	the identity component then refines this to the degree-zero subfunctor on the
81	\'etale sheafification). This moduli interpretation is what underlies the
82	Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) of
83	\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. The universal homomorphism $\psi \colon J
84	\to A\( corresponding to a pointed morphism \)\varphi \colon C \to A$ is
85	constructed by Milne's symmetric-power route: the symmetric assignment
86	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\) defines a
87	regular morphism \(\widetilde \psi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\), the
88	birational morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J\) inverts to a
89	rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\), and the rational-map extension theorem
90	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes that rational map to the
91	desired regular morphism \(\psi\).
92	
93	The goal of the chapter is to establish:
94	
95	\begin{theorem}
96	
97	\leanok
98	[Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) -- Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1]
99	  \label{thm:albanese_universal_property}
100	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.albanese_universal_property}
101	  
102	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, doc p.~104
103	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
104	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
105	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let \(P\) be a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\).  The map
106	  %  \(f^P\colon C\,\to\,J\) has the following universal property: for any
107	  %  map \(\varphi\colon C\to A\) from \(C\) into an abelian variety sending
108	  %  \(P\) to \(0\), there is a unique homomorphism \(\psi\colon J\to A\) such
109	  %  that \(\varphi=\psi\circ f^P\)."
110	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, p.~104.}
111	
112	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over an algebraically
113	  closed field \(\bar k\), let \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a \(\bar k\)-rational point,
114	  and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\), \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) for the
115	  Abel--Jacobi morphism of \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. Then for every
116	  abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) and every pointed morphism
117	  \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) with \(\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\), there exists a unique
118	  homomorphism of group schemes \(\psi \colon J \to A\) such that
119	  \(\varphi = \psi \circ \iota_{P_0}\).
120	\end{theorem}
121	
122	The rest of this chapter develops the sub-lemmas
123	(\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}, \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
124	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map}, \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian},
125	and \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}) that the proof rests on,
126	then gives the proof.
127	
128	\section{The Abel--Jacobi morphism at a marked point}
129	\label{sec:albaneseup_abel_jacobi}
130	
131	The first sub-lemma packages the construction and the elementary regularity of
132	the Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0}\) at the basepoint \(P_0\). It is the
133	content gated on A.3 (existence of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a representable group
134	scheme).

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
26	base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).

36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).

120	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
121	  (Definition~REF). There exists a scheme
122	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
123	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
124	  \begin{enumerate}
125	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
126	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
127	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
128	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
129	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
130	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
131	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
132	  \end{enumerate}

134	  over \(X\).

152	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps

154	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
155	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
156	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
157	\end{proof}
